author: Corné POTGIETER

title: I Built a Tiny Computer Inside a Transformer
content:
By compiling a simple program directly into transformer weights.

The post I Built a Tiny Computer Inside a Transformer appeared first on Towards Data Science.
author: Sean Moran

title: From Fuzzy Global to Clear Local: A Focus and Super-Resolution-Guided Tiny Target Detection Method for Full-Scene Images
content: Detecting tiny targets, a fundamental satellite observation task, always suffers from information loss, additional computation costs, and imbalanced samples in full-scene imagery. However, generic methods rely on complex deep neural networks to traverse every region of the image, irrespective of the nonuniform target distribution, severely hindering the detection accuracy and leading to a heavy computation burden. This article proposes a feature focusing super-resolution small target detection network (FFSSTD-Net), alleviating the above three problems. First, the proposed convolution focus detection (CFD) module uses a lightweight full-convolutional network query mechanism to adaptively search the regions with targets, eliminating unnecessary computation costs and noise from the background. Then, the proposed feature super-resolution (FSR) module guides the backbone network, enhancing the representation of small target objects in high-dimensional features through an auxiliary branch to achieve a balanced optimization, alleviating the information loss caused by sub-sampling operations. The experimental results demonstrate that the FFSSTD-Net outperforms state-of-the-art generic methods, improving mAP and Fps by at least 3.14% and 10%, respectively. Meanwhile, ablation experiments indicate that the CFD and FSR modules enhance detection accuracy while accelerating full-scene image processing.
author: Yucong He, Gui Gao, Zhenghuan Xia, Dunyun He, Gang Yang, Xi Zhang, Gaosheng Li

title: Calibration of the Spaceborne GNSS-R Specular Delay Tracking on Fengyun-3 Missions and Its Impact on Ocean Wind Retrieval
content: Calibration is one of the foundations of spaceborne Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) geophysical parameter retrievals. This article highlights that, besides power calibration, accurate time delay calibration is also crucial to GNSS-R ocean wind retrievals as the computation of normalized bistatic radar cross section (NBRCS) requires the location of the specular point (SP) in the delay–Doppler map (DDM). First, the specular delay tracking algorithm of Fengyun-3 (FY-3) GNSS-R missions is described, which particularly considers the geoid undulation and land topography. The sources and impacts of the specular delay error are discussed. Then, three methods that evaluate and calibrate the specular delay in the FY-3E and FY-3F in-orbit data are proposed: by sea water observations, sea ice observations, and waveform retracking. Results from the three methods agree with each other, finding a difference in the specular delay tracking between FY-3E and FY-3F, which also differs for different GNSS systems. The calibration by sea ice observations was found to be the optimal method. Finally, the impacts of delay errors on the ocean wind retrieval are quantified by actual observations and simulations. A bias in the specular delay could cause several issues, such as NBRCS inconsistency and a decrease in wind retrieval accuracy, depending on the magnitude and sign of the bias.
author: Feixiong Huang, Junming Xia, Wenqiang Lu, Cong Yin, Dongwei Wang, Cheng Liu, Xiaochun Zhai, Xianyi Wang, Yusen Tian, Qifei Du, Yueqiang Sun, Weihua Bai, Na Xu, Xiuqing Hu, Jian Shang

title: Characterizing Shale Brittleness and Organic Enrichment Through Matrix Modulus Using Rock Physics-Based Elastic Impedance Inversion
content: Deep shale gas, as a crucial unconventional energy resource, is assuming an increasingly significant role in securing energy supply and advancing the global shift toward low-carbon development. Nevertheless, deep shale reservoirs are typically characterized by pronounced heterogeneity, complex mineralogical composition, and significant organic enrichment, which make the simultaneous seismic-scale characterization of brittleness and organic matter particularly challenging. To overcome these difficulties, we present a rock physics-driven inversion framework that incorporates two diagnostic parameters: the matrix bulk modulus ( $K_{m}$ ) and the disturbance bulk modulus ( $\Delta K$ ).  $K_{m}$  characterizes the inherent stiffness of the mineral matrix and demonstrates strong sensitivity to brittle mineral abundance, whereas  $\Delta K$  captures the softening impact of organic matter on rock rigidity, thereby offering improved sensitivity to organic enrichment. By combining these two parameters, the respective influences of minerals and organic matter can be effectively disentangled at the seismic scale. Building upon this concept, we derive a new reflection coefficient expression formulated in terms of the matrix modulus and establish an elastic impedance (EI) inversion scheme. This framework enables the concurrent prediction of shale brittleness and organic enrichment from seismic observations. Rock physics analysis confirms that  $K_{m}$  is highly sensitive to brittle mineral content, while  $\Delta K$  exhibits a relative increase of up to 300% with rising organic matter fraction, significantly exceeding the response of conventional density (~20%) and P-wave impedance (~40%). Synthetic tests show over 95% consistency between the proposed reflectivity and the exact Zoeppritz solution within 30° incidence. Well-log validation yields correlation coefficients of 0.77 between  $K_{m}$  and mineral brittleness index, and 0.81 between  $\Delta K$  and organic matter content, both outperforming conventional elastic indicators. Field applications demonstrate that the method achieves higher prediction accuracy, better vertical resolution, and improved lateral continuity compared to existing techniques. Beyond shale gas, this methodology offers a broadly applicable framework for characterizing unconventional reservoirs, including shale oil, oil shale, tight hydrocarbons, and gas hydrates, highlighting its relevance for future energy exploration and development.
author: Xiaoyu Lv, Zhentao Sun, Huafeng Hu, Zhaoyun Zong

title: SAM-Based Multiscale Fine-Tuning With Interlayer Difference Guidance for Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: Visual foundation models (VFMs) have attracted growing interest in recent years. However, a significant domain gap between natural and remote sensing imagery—particularly in terms of scale variation—remains a core challenge in remote sensing change detection (CD). In addition, many existing methods rely on single-level difference features and lack the ability to propagate change representations across network layers. To address these limitations, we propose IDRG-mSAM, a segment anything model (SAM)-based multiscale fine-tuning framework with interlayer difference representation and guidance, for remote sensing CD. Our approach first leverages the SAM as a general-purpose backbone, utilizing its strong capacity for dense prediction tasks. To bridge the domain gap and capture multiscale context, we introduce a multiscale adapter, which enhances feature expressiveness by modeling contextual dependencies at various spatial resolutions. Moreover, we design an Interlayer Difference Representation and Guidance Module to facilitate progressive interaction of bitemporal features. This module propagates difference cues across network depths, enhancing multilevel difference representation and improving the quality of bitemporal image features. Extensive experiments on three widely used benchmark datasets, LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and CLCD, demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our method, achieving consistently superior performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches.
author: Leiquan Wang, Yu Meng, Chunbo Luo, Mingming Xu, Chunlei Wu, Zhongwei Li

title: Climate-Driven Glacier Retreat and Biocrust Colonization Dynamics in the Quelccaya Ice Cap, Peru
content: The spatiotemporal dynamics of glacier cover and biocrust surrounding the Quelccaya Ice Cap in the Peruvian Andes from 2020 to 2024 were analyzed. Using Sentinel-2 satellite imagery, the adjusted normalized difference snow index (ANDSI) and normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) were employed with the K-means machine learning algorithm to generate land cover time-series maps. TerraClimate dataset provided monthly estimates of climate and water balance variables, which were analyzed to identify the drivers of seasonal land cover transitions. The results indicate that the climate variables influenced the seasonal patterns of land cover. A reduction of 4.74 $\text{km}^{2}$ in the ice cover area and 7.48 $\text{km}^{2}$ in the snow cover area was observed between June 2020 and June 2024, primarily influenced by high incoming shortwave radiation, which increased air temperatures, evaporation, and the sublimation process. Biocrust occurs predominantly in the north and west of the ice cap, particularly in flat relief areas, while wetlands occur at lower altitudes. Both vegetation types are highly sensitive to environmental shifts, showing seasonal growth associated with higher temperatures and increased water availability. The top-of-atmosphere (TOA) imagery can be used in a clustering process to generate land cover maps. The combination of ANDSI and NDVI proved effective in mapping ice, snow, biocrust, and wetlands using Sentinel-2 TOA reflectance images, thereby avoiding the relief shadow effects typically observed in the clustering process.
author: Eliana Lima da Fonseca, Tiago Pereira Bettervide, Jefferson Cardia Simões

title: Classification From Airborne Traditional Point Cloud to Multispectral Point Cloud: A Review
content: Airborne point cloud processing has become a core research focus within the fields of geoinformation science and remote sensing, attracting significant attention in recent years. Concurrently, with the rapid advancement of sensor technology, airborne point cloud processing is undergoing a technological leap from single-band to multispectral systems. Consequently, research in point cloud classification has flourished, with numerous methods emerging to address diverse challenges within this domain. However, the inherent characteristics of both traditional airborne laser scanning (ALS) point clouds and multispectral point clouds present unique challenges for classification tasks. To advance future research, this article provides a comprehensive and systematic review of state-of-the-art methods for traditional ALS point cloud classification. It also offers a forward-looking summary of the latest and most advanced developments in multispectral point cloud classification learning, outlining the cutting-edge technological landscape for readers. Comparative experimental results are presented across multiple datasets, alongside profound insights, and suggestive directions for future research.
author: Qinge Wang, Jianling Kuang, Zhengang Zhao, Tao Shen, Qingwang Wang

title: Multisensor Fusion for Monitoring Shorelines and Coastal Environments: A Review and Perspective With Satellite Imagery
content: Satellite-derived remote sensing has emerged as an indispensable tool for monitoring dynamic shorelines and coastal environments. However, persistent challenges remain with single-satellite adoptions, including limited spatial resolutions, spectral constraints, low revisit cycles, and atmospheric interferences, which hinder their efficacy. This review summarizes recent advancements in multisatellite fusion, which integrates data derived from different available satellites that can be equipped with multispectral or hyperspectral cameras, synthetic aperture radar (SAR), and light detection and ranging (LiDAR), to enhance accuracy and reliability in coastal monitoring. New strategies that synergize pixel-level and feature-level fusion techniques can address critical challenges in coastal remote sensing. Improvements in pixel-level fusion techniques, such as pan-sharpening, can also significantly enhance spatial resolution by merging high-resolution commercial satellites (e.g., WorldView-3, PlanetScope, SPOT) with lower-resolution public satellites (e.g., Sentinel-2, Landsat). Complementary feature-level fusion techniqueshelp to perceive contextual integrity through spectral indices, texture analysis, and machine learning. The synergistic integration of cloud-penetrating SAR (e.g., Sentinel-1) and precise LiDAR (e.g., ICESat-2) with optical data (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2) further enables all-weather monitoring capabilities, particularly valuable for tropical coasts with persistent cloud cover. These advancements in multisensor fusion highlight the potential for further precise and scalable satellite-based coastal monitoring, which can aid in climate-resilient solutions for sustainable coastal management.
author: Ha Linh Trinh, Jing Xian Lim, Hieu Trung Kieu, Jenn Wei Er, Dawn Sok Cheng Pang, Adrian Wing-Keung Law

title: Lightweight Remote Sensing Multispectral Image Compression With Decoupled Interactive Knowledge Distillation
content: To address the challenges of spectral and spatial redundancy in multispectral images, as well as the limitations of satellite-to-ground bandwidth and onboard computational resources, a lightweight multispectral image compression with decoupled interactive knowledge distillation framework, MIC-DIKD, is proposed in this particleaper. Within the encoder–decoder backbone, a multiscale spatial–spectral redundancy reduction block and a decoupled dual-branch attention module are introduced. Cross-band and cross-spatial dependencies are jointly modeled. Redundancy is suppressed at the source, and representational capacity is enhanced. Dual constraints at the pixel and feature levels are further applied. The student model is guided toward the teacher. Strong rate–distortion performance and perceptual quality are achieved. Experiments on Landsat-8 and Sentinel-2 show that MIC-DIKD outperforms several baselines in peak signal-to-noise ratio, multiscale structural similarity, and mean spectral angle. In terms of complexity, the student model distilled from the teacher provides comparable performance. FLOPs and encoding/decoding latency are substantially reduced. The framework is therefore well suited to the engineering requirements of onboard compression and ground-based fast decoding. Overall, a practical solution is provided for multispectral image onboard compression, ground decoding, and large-scale processing. High performance is maintained, and complexity is reduced.
author: Qizhi Fang, Jingang Wang, Jiahui Liu, Lili Zhang

title: Balanced Motions Decomposition From Sea Surface Height Using Transformer-Based UNet Model
content: Separating balanced motions (BM) from unbalanced motions (UBM) in single sea surface height (SSH) snapshots is crucial for maximizing Surface Water and Ocean Topography (SWOT) altimeter returns, but challenging due to undersampled internal-wave signals. Here, we introduce Tr-UNet, a transformer-augmented UNet that leverages self-attention to capture both local and global spatial dependencies in sea surface height (SSH) images, thereby enabling the potential isolation of BM from the composite field. To train and evaluate the network, we constructed the first globally distributed training sets comprising regions with strong and weak UBM, extracted from a high-resolution realistic simulation. Model performance is evaluated in three experiments, including strong, weak, and mixed UBM scenarios, which, respectively, hold out energetic, weak, and combined regions for blind testing. Across all cases, Tr-UNet delivers the lowest root-mean-square error (RMSE) and mean absolute error (MAE), reducing RMSE by up to 40% compared to UNet variants, AutoEncoder and Guassian filter, and preserving centimeter-level accuracy. Gridwise and daywise diagnostics show that Tr-UNet halves the spread of prediction errors compared to the next-best model, demonstrating superior spatial and temporal robustness. These results show that embedding transformer blocks within a convolutional encoder–decoder markedly improves the generalization capability of deep-learning SSH decomposers, providing a practical, data-driven route for real-time BM retrieval from SWOT and future high-resolution altimeters. Nonetheless, Tr-UNet is a purely data-driven model trained on numerical simulations and does not explicitly enforce physical constraints such as exact additivity between the decomposed balanced and unbalanced components and the total SSH, so the retrieved fields should be interpreted as approximate diagnostics rather than exact dynamical partitions.
author: Xiaoxiong Zhang, Guoqiang Liu, Fahim Abdul Gafoor, Yacine Addad, Changming Dong, Maryam Rashed AlShehhi

title: Geometric Accuracy Analysis of Geostationary Environment Monitoring Spectrometer Images by Spatiotemporal and Spectral Collocation
content: Geometric accuracy of satellite imagery is a critical factor that determines the reliability of image analysis. Geostationary environment monitoring spectrometer (GEMS) is the world's first geostationary hyperspectral environmental satellite launched in 2020. Unique geometric and radiometric characteristics of GEMS make conventional techniques difficult to be applied for automated geometric accuracy evaluation of GEMS images. This article aims to handle this problem by matching GEMS images with images from advanced meteorological imager (AMI), a meteorological geostationary satellite. Since GEMS and AMI differ in scan patterns and spectral responses, their spatiotemporal and spectral characteristics are different. For accurate matching, we apply collocation techniques to make these characteristics conform to each other. Geometric accuracy was evaluated through global and local matching. Global matching results showed that geometric accuracy of GEMS images exhibited a repetitive error trend of about 1 pixel in the north–south direction over time. Local matching enabled high-density analysis of detailed error distribution and direction across the entire image. Furthermore, comparison of results with and without collocation revealed that excluding spectral collocation increased irregular errors across the image. Without strict temporal collocation, geometric errors grew in regions strongly affected by observation time differences. In contrast, when spatial, spectral, and strict temporal collocation were all applied, the average root mean square error remained stable at about 1–1.5 pixels. These findings demonstrate that the collocation process is an essential preprocessing step for evaluating geometric accuracy of geostationary hyperspectral satellites such as GEMS. Future article will expand toward developing algorithms to improve geometric accuracy of the GEMS based on these results.
author: Seunghyeok Choi, Taejung Kim, Jaehoon Jeong, Jeonghyeon Seo, Kyunghwa Lee

title: Beyond Pretty Pictures: Combined Single- and Multiimage Super-Resolution for Sentinel-2 Images
content: Super-resolution aims to increase the resolution of satellite images by reconstructing high-frequency details, which go beyond naïve upsampling. This has particular relevance for Earth observation missions like Sentinel-2, which offer frequent, regular coverage at no cost; but at coarse resolution. Its pixel footprint is too large to capture small features, such as houses, streets, and hedge rows. To address this, we present Sen4x, a hybrid super-resolution architecture that combines the advantages of single-image and multi-image techniques. It combines temporal oversampling from repeated Sentinel-2 acquisitions with a learned prior from high-resolution Pléiades Neo data. In doing so, Sen4x upgrades Sentinel-2 imagery to 2.5 m ground sampling distance. We test the super-resolved images on urban land-cover classification in Hanoi, Vietnam. We find that they lead to a significant performance improvement over state-of-the-art super-resolution baselines.
author: Aditya Retnanto, Son Le, Sebastian Mueller, Armin Leitner, Michael Riffler, Konrad Schindler, Yohan Iddawela

title: Development of a Global Climate Model for Atmospheric Temperature Using Machine Learning
content: This article presents a novel three-dimensional global model of atmospheric temperature developed using Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) trained on radio occultation (RO) data from the COSMIC I and COSMIC II satellite missions. Over 14.7 million quality-controlled profiles were used, providing approximately 9.5 billion data points that capture temperature variability across latitude, longitude, altitude (0–60 km), and time (2006–2025). The global domain was divided into 1296 spatial grid cells (10° × 5°) to enable localized ANN training and ensure efficient handling of regional atmospheric dynamics. Model performance was evaluated through cross-validation and independent testing against radiosonde measurements from 684 stations worldwide. Results show mean absolute errors of 1.5 °C–4.5 °C and root-mean-square errors of 2.5 °C–6.5 °C, with best performance in the tropical troposphere and increasing errors toward high latitudes. The model successfully reproduces key climatological structures (including the tropospheric lapse rate, stratospheric inversion, and seasonal hemispheric asymmetries), and accurately captures diurnal and annual thermal cycles. Long-term simulations (2006–2025) reveal a distinct tropospheric warming trend (∼+0.07 °C per year at 11 km) and a corresponding stratospheric cooling (∼−0.03 °C per year near 32 km), consistent with established satellite and reanalysis records. These results demonstrate that ANN-based frameworks can effectively model global atmospheric thermal structure and evolution, providing a scalable approach for future climate monitoring and forecasting applications.
author: Daniel Okoh, Adero Awuor, George Ochieng, Paul Baki, John Bosco Habarulema, Babatunde Rabiu, Punyawi Jamjareegulgarn, Loretta Onuorah, Jonas Nzeagwu, Aderonke Akerele, Gabriel Friday Ibeh, David Omole, James Ameh

title: Modality-Invariant Optical–SAR Image Registration Using Hierarchical Structural Cognition Prior
content: Existing deep learning methods for optical and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) image registration have attempted to incorporate structural or gradient information. Although capable of simultaneously extracting low-level structural features and high-level semantic features, most still primarily rely on grayscale or intensity information. As a result, explicit guidance for accurately modeling structural features within the network is often limited. Under cross-modal conditions characterized by strong nonlinear radiometric variations and noise interference, these limitations in feature description partially constrain registration performance. To address this issue, this article proposes a registration framework guided by hierarchical structural information. We introduce a structure-aware weight extraction module to explicitly capture multiscale edge and curvature features. By integrating channel and spatial attention mechanisms, we translate structural priors into guidance for the feature learning process. Furthermore, within the joint detection–description coupled constraint framework, we introduce a differentiated edge prior information computation strategy and embed it into the constraints of keypoint detection to improve structural consistency constraints under cross-modal conditions. Concurrently, we design an enhanced description alignment loss that leverages multilevel constraints to enhance the learning capability of cross-modal similarity features. Finally, matching and registration performance comparisons with other algorithms were conducted on two datasets. The experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves effective improvements in enhancing the robustness and accuracy of optical–SAR image registration.
author: Hengxiao Jia, Fan Zhang, Yanming Chen, Qiang Yin, Fei Ma

title: DuFANet: A Multisource Remote Sensing Image Classification Method Integrating Frequency-Domain Attention and Cross-Modal Transformers
content: Jointclassification of hyperspectral imagery with LiDAR and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) multimodal remote sensing data holds significant value for enhancing feature recognition accuracy. However, it still faces challenges, such as severe spectral interference, modal imbalance, and insufficient feature fusion. To address these challenges, this article proposes a multisource fusion network—DuFANet. This approach introduces a frequency-domain attention module in the hyperspectral branch to explicitly decompose and enhance spectral–spatial features. In the LiDAR/SAR branch, a depthwise convolutional self-attention module is designed to efficiently model modal local geometric features and global contextual information. A parallel transformer encoder is employed after both hyperspectral and LiDAR/SAR branches to capture long-range dependencies and global semantics. The fusion stage introduces a dual-branch cross-modal fusion attention module, achieving stable complementarity through dual-layer mechanisms at both the channel and spatial dimensions. Experiments on five datasets—Berlin, Muufl, Houston2013, Augsburg, and Trento, DuFANet achieves overall classification accuracies of 79.29%, 92.31%, 99.44%, 97.59%, and 99.75%, respectively, significantly outperforming existing methods. Furthermore, the model demonstrates exceptional performance in complex urban scenes and under few-shot conditions, achieving a favorable balance between accuracy and computational cost.
author: Yong Mei, Xiangsuo Fan, Jinlong Fan

title: FEDA-Net: A Feature Enhancement and Dynamic Alignment Network for SAR Ship Detection
content: Due to the imaging mechanism of synthetic aperture radar (SAR), speckle noise often obscures details of the ship and leads to the loss of semantic information. Meanwhile, the presence of targets at extremely diverse scales challenges the capacity of single-feature representation. In addition, geometric deformations of ships tend to induce localization inaccuracies and reduce classification confidence. These issues collectively aggravate the competition and imbalance between classification and localization tasks. In response to these challenges, we propose a feature enhancement and dynamic alignment network (FEDA-Net) to improve ship detection in SAR images. First, a lightweight co-attention network (LCA-Net) is designed, which synergistically captures both local and global multiscale features through a multilevel convolutional architecture and a dual-dimensional attention mechanism, thereby enhancing the extraction of noise-robust multiscale features. Second, a cross-stage fusion pyramid network (CSFPN) is constructed to achieve complementary integration of semantic and detailed information via bidirectional feature fusion. During training, the fusion module adopts a multibranch topology to enrich multiscale feature information, while during inference, it is converted into a single-branch structure through structural re-parameterization, effectively reducing parameter redundancy. Finally, we innovatively propose a dynamic alignment detection head, which adopts parameter sharing and adaptive task-decoupling strategies to separate the classification and localization branches, while incorporating a bidirectional interaction mechanism to effectively balance the competition between the two tasks. On the SSDD and HRSID datasets, the proposed method achieves accuracies of 99.1% and 93.2%, respectively, while achieving a reduction in model parameters. Furthermore, the model demonstrates superior generalization performance on the large-scale LS-SSDD-v1.0 dataset.
author: Shuaiqi Liu, Yue Yu, Ruiyi Feng, Bing Li, Yudong Zhang, Qi Hu

title: Dual Low-Rank Constrained Self-Representation Learning for Unsupervised SAR Image Change Detection
content: Change detection (CD) in bitemporal synthetic aperture radar images has been an important research direction in remote sensing intelligent interpretation. Recently, feature matrix decomposition methods based on superpixel analysis have demonstrated significant advantages in reducing computational complexity and suppressing detail noise by expanding the scale of analysis units. However, existing low-rank and sparse decomposition models focus solely on the low-rank structure among samples while neglecting intrinsic correlations in the feature space. Moreover, in fragmented and irregular change regions, the single sample-level low-rank assumption often fails, leading to insufficient geometric structure preservation and significant decomposition deviations. To address these issues, we propose a self-representation learning framework based on dual low-rank constraints. The framework simultaneously decomposes the change feature matrix into a projection matrix representing feature correlations and an affinity matrix describing sample similarities, while imposing global low-rank constraints on both matrices to achieve collaborative modeling of sample and feature spaces. By introducing a projection distance penalty and Laplacian local correlation constraints, the model effectively enhances its capability to characterize local geometric structures. Based on the reconstructed difference images, we further design an adaptive manifold regularization graph optimization algorithm that transforms the binary classification problem into a graph partitioning optimization problem, improving classification accuracy. Experiments on seven benchmark datasets containing complex disaster scenarios, such as tsunamis and wildfires, demonstrate the superior performance of our method in detecting complex change regions, achieving an average Kappa coefficient improvement of 3.83% compared to state-of-the-art methods. The source code will be available online.
author: Weisong Li, Yinwei Li, Yiming Zhu

title: Bi-Layer Cartesian Back-Projection Focusing Algorithm and Airborne Radio Echo Sounding Application for Antarctic Subglacial Structure Imaging
content: Radio echo sounding (RES), a powerful remote geophysical technique, provides excellent insight into the characterization of subglacial environment. However, existing time-domain RES imaging methods face challenges in balancing computational efficiency and imaging accuracy for along-track focusing of RES data. In this study, we propose a bi-layer Cartesian back-projection (BCBP) algorithm for efficient and accurate imaging of subglacial structures, which incorporates the newly derived medium-based spectrum compression within an interpolation-free imaging framework. The proposed approach inherently accounts for refractive effects in the Antarctic bi-layer imaging scenario and effectively mitigates spectrum aliasing under low-sampling subglacial conditions. It thereby eliminates the need for spatial interpolation during sub-image fusion to achieve accelerated computation without sacrificing focusing quality. The algorithm is validated by point-target simulation of a classical ice geometry and further applied to airborne RES data from two representative regions of Antarctica: The interior Gamburtsev region and the coastal fringe of Totten Glacier. Both simulation and field RES results demonstrate that the proposed BCBP algorithm achieves high-resolution focusing comparable to the benchmarking BP, while offering substantially improved computational efficiency. The successful validation of the BCBP algorithm underscores its potential for wide application in precise subglacial imaging by efficiently reprocessing existing RES data, as well as new data to be acquired in the coming years in Antarctica.
author: Chen Lv, Wenhao Luo, Kenichi Matsuoka, Tong Hao

title: HiMamba2: Hierarchical State Space Modeling With Linear Complexity for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Hyperspectral image classification faces persistent challenges arising from high spectral dimensionality, complex spatial-spectral dependencies, and limited labeled samples. Although Transformer-based methods demonstrate strong long-range modeling capability, their quadratic computational complexity O(L2·d) imposes substantial bottlenecks for high-dimensional data. State space model (SSM) approaches achieve linear complexity O(L·d) but predominantly employ single-scale feature extraction and lack effective regularization mechanisms. This article proposes HiMamba2, a hierarchical SSM architecture incorporating three principal contributions: parallel multiscale convolutional embedding that captures complementary spectral-spatial representations across multiple receptive field scales; asymmetric weighted residual connections combined with adaptive DropPath regularization and serial convolutional block attention module dual-attention to enhance training stability and feature discriminability under limited labeled data conditions; an asymmetric dual-stage design with cross-layer fusion that integrates shallow fine-grained features with deep semantic representations while preserving linear complexity O(L·d). Experiments on Houston2013, WHU-Hi-HanChuan, and Pavia University datasets demonstrate overall accuracies of 93.40%, 93.02%, and 97.52%, respectively, outperforming 13 representative methods spanning CNN, attention, Transformer, and Mamba-based architectures. Training requires 262.84 s for 116-epoch convergence with 182.95 MB peak GPU memory consumption and 0.665 ms per-sample inference, validating the practical efficiency of the proposed approach. The complete PyTorch implementation of HiMamba2 will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Jason20155/ upon acceptance of this manuscript.
author: Jianshang Liao, Liguo Wang

title: MTSNet: A Dual-Branch Geometric Feature Aggregation Network for Mapping Single Tree Stems From Terrestrial Laser Scanning Point Clouds
content: Accurately mapping tree stems is essential for the analysis and estimation of tree parameters derived from terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) point clouds, including critical measurements, such as diameter at breast height (DBH), wood volume, and above-ground biomass. Traditional methods often struggle to address the variability in stem size and morphology across different tree species and face challenges in distinguishing tree stems from branches, particularly in complex structural environments. To address these challenges, we propose MTSNet, a deep learning network designed for mapping tree stems across multiple species using TLS point clouds, which incorporates the dual-branch hybrid geometric contextual feature extraction (HGCFE) module and the multilevel feature adaptive aggregation (MFAA) module. The HGCFE module leverages a dual-branch architecture to fully exploit local geometric relationships: one branch employs dynamic weight kernels to model the varying stem shapes across different species, while the other branch captures local mesh topology to enhance the network’s ability to distinguish tree stems from branches at intersections. The MFAA module aims to adaptively fuse various types of geometric features at multiple levels, which introduce confidence scores to automatically balance the contributions of different geometric features, thereby facilitating effective multilevel feature aggregation. Experiments are conducted on TLS point cloud datasets featuring 11 tree species with diverse structures and shapes, which validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method for stem mapping across different species. Furthermore, key structural parameters related to tree stems, such as DBH, are estimated. The results demonstrate that incorporating stem mapping significantly improves the accuracy of DBH estimation.
author: Xijian Fan, Changwei Bi, Heng Zhang, Yanjie Li, Tardi Tjahjadi, Miaomiao Zhang

title: DSCD: A Dual-Stream Cross-Domain Network for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: The domain adaptation method for cross-domain classification alleviates the dependence of deep learning on a large amount of labeled data in hyperspectral image classification, but it still requires a relatively adequate number of labeled samples from the source domain to achieve stable transfer effects. Meanwhile, cross-domain few-shot classification is constrained by distribution discrepancies and category mismatches between source and target domains. To address this challenge, this article proposes a dual-stream cross-domain network (DSCD) based on existing few-shot networks, aiming to explore how to effectively transfer source-domain knowledge to improve learning efficiency in the target domain under limited annotations. Within a metalearning paradigm, DSCD integrates feature discriminability and domain adaptability through a two-stage collaborative optimization mechanism to improve cross-domain few-shot classification performance. In the first stage, a spectral–spatial weighting module is designed by combining a spectral–spatial joint attention mechanism with depthwise separable convolutions, which enhances feature discriminability while simplifying the model structure. In the second stage, a feature embedding module (FEM) is developed to leverage the complementary strengths of 2-D and 3-D convolutions. Utilizing octave convolution, FEM facilitates a dual-path interaction between low- and high-frequency components, along with the interfrequency feature exchange mechanism, enabling deep mining of spectral–spatial representations. Experiments on four benchmark datasets—Pavia Center, Pavia University, Salinas, and Indian Pines—demonstrate that DSCD achieves the overall classification accuracies of 97.49%, 82.59%, 92.02%, and 77.89%, respectively. Furthermore, all experiments were conducted under a cross-domain one-shot few-shot setting, where only one labeled sample per category in the target domain was provided for model adaptation. These results indicate that DSCD not only outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods but also exhibits strong generalization and cross-domain adaptability.
author: Yulin Cai, Zhipeng Chang, Lukang Wang, Xinglu Wang, Wenlu Liu, Yue Zhuo

title: Mapping Present-Day Vertical Land Motion in the Alpine–Apennine System from GNSS Time Series: Uncertainty Reduction Using Hydrological Loading Corrections
content: Understanding vertical land motion (VLM) in the Alpine–Apennine orogenic system requires disentangling tectonic drivers from surface mass redistribution. This study presents a multigeodetic analysis of contemporary VLM in the Alpine–Apennine orogenic system by integrating global navigation satellite system (GNSS) (2002–2021), gravity recovery and climate experiment and follow-on (GRACE/GRACE-FO), and hydrological models, covering the time frame from 2002 to 2021. We resolve critical uncertainties through three corrections: 1) Common mode error, reduced by ∼30% in velocity uncertainty via principal component analysis, primarily associated with 2–7 year hydrological interannual signals; 2) hydrology-induced elastic deformation, contributing an average of –0.3 mm/yr to GNSS-derived VLM, with GRACE/GRACE-FO corrections suppressing uplift rates by 0.3 mm/yr in the Alps; 3) glacial isostatic adjustment, exhibiting a southward-decreasing trend (–0.1 to –0.5 mm/yr) from the Alps to Apennines. Beyond a multidataset comparison, this study provides a reproducible framework to reduce the uncertainty of GNSS-derived VLM by integrating physically based surface-mass-loading corrections with a consistent noise-aware uncertainty assessment. We quantify how much of the apparent VLM scatter can be explained by hydrological loading and identify the residual components that remain dominated by colored noise and nonloading local effects. The VLM imaging reveals coherent regional-scale deformation domains across the Alpine–Apennine system (Alpine uplift, Po Plain subsidence, and alternating uplift/subsidence along the Apennines), enabling a spatially interpretable characterization of contemporary VLM rather than site-by-site reporting. The proposed workflow is transferable to other regions and provides practical guidance on when loading corrections are expected to be most effective and where limitations persist.
author: Meilin He, Yuanjin Pan, Lv Zhou, Jiashuang Jiao, Tao Chen, Yixin Xiao

title: Deep Learning-Based Automated Coastline Extraction From Satellite Imagery: A Sentinel-2 Benchmark Dataset Approach
content: Existing coastline extraction methods from satellite imagery primarily detect instantaneous water edges, resulting in coastlines that are highly sensitive to tidal fluctuations and often inconsistent with the geomorphological definitions required for long-term coastal analysis. This conceptual limitation is further compounded by the scarcity of open-access, large-scale, and geomorphologically diverse labeled datasets, which hinders the development, benchmarking, and generalization of deep learning models across heterogeneous coastal environments. To address this gap, we developed the Sentinel-2 coastline training dataset (SCTD), eliminating the need for traditional water-edge detection and tidal correction steps, comprising 1525 multiscenario image-label pairs (predominantly sandy coastlines) that cover various landforms including artificial islands, river channels, lagoons, small fishing ports, and spits. In addition, we proposed a hybrid adaptive loss function with edge constraints, whose performance is comparable to other commonly used loss functions, and which further enhance the capability of deep learning models to directly extract coastlines. Six U-Net-based convolutional models were trained and evaluated using a combination of quantitative metrics and qualitative analyses. The results demonstrate that coastline detection performance is governed by the coupled interaction of network architecture, loss function design, and geomorphological type, rather than by model structure alone. The SCTD dataset and supporting code are publicly available, establishing a benchmark for coastline extraction in remote sensing and machine learning. This approach enables accurate monitoring of coastal erosion, assessment of engineering interventions, and support for climate adaptation planning. By integrating multisource and higher resolution data, the framework can be extended to larger regions and more complex coastal types, representing a paradigm shift from instantaneous waterline detection to the extraction of geomorphologically meaningful “true coastlines.”
author: Taihuan Hu, Shenliang Chen, Yin Cao, Hongyu Ji, Zipeng Gu, Jiarui Zhang, Yanqing Wei

title: Dynamic Neural Architecture-Driven Spatial Semantic Modeling and Multiscale Collaborative Sensing of Remote Sensing Objects
content: Aiming at the severe challenges to the accuracy and robustness of the detection model caused by the complexity problems, such as high object density, dramatic scale changes, occlusion, variable illumination and class imbalance in remote sensing image object detection, this study innovatively proposes a multiscale adaptive remote sensing network (MARS-Net). First, the spatial context network (SCNet) is constructed. The adaptive feature extraction of complex object morphology is realized by the conditional convolution module, which significantly improves the adaptability of the model to diverse objects in remote sensing images. At the same time, SCNet integrates the shift window transformer module to effectively capture the long-distance dependencies between objects and enhance the model’s semantic understanding of complex scenes. In addition, an enhanced multiscale feature pyramid network (EMFPN) is designed. The core of EMFPN is the global-offset and detail aggregation module, which integrates the low-stage feature alignment module and the high-stage feature alignment module of the gold module. The simple aggregation module is used to efficiently fuse multiscale information from different feature layers, thereby improving the feature utilization efficiency. The low-stage information fusion module and the high-stage information fusion module use omni-dimensional dynamic convolution to more comprehensively model the spatial information of the object and accurately process the direction and deformation of the object. The high-stage information fusion module also incorporates the exponential moving average attention mechanism to dynamically adjust the attention weight of features, further enhancing the discriminative ability of key features and the robustness of the overall model. The experimental results show that MARS-Net improves mAP@50 by 3.38% and 1.21% on RSOD and DOTAv2.0 datasets, and mAP@50:95 by 14.61% and 0.55%, respectively, which fully verifies its superior performance and practical application potential in complex remote sensing scenarios.
author: Chenshuai Bai, Kaijun Wu, Xiaofeng Bai, Xiaoqiang Wu

title: GLMamba: A Global–Local Mamba Network for Efficient Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: With the rapid advancement of deep learning, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Transformers have become two mainstream architectures for remote sensing change detection (CD). CNNs are effective at extracting local features but struggle to model long-range dependencies due to limited receptive fields. Transformers capture global contextual information through self-attention, yet their high computational cost limits their practicality for high-resolution remote sensing imagery. To balance global modeling capability and local detail representation, this article introduces the Mamba architecture into CD and proposes global–local Mamba (GLMamba), an efficient network capable of jointly modeling long-range dependencies and fine-grained spatial details. Built upon the state-space model, GLMamba establishes a unified global–local modeling framework. In the encoding stage, the network extracts global features via the state space structure and enhances multiscale semantic aggregation through the Change-aware Semantic Aggregation Module. The Bitemporal Fusion Attention (BFA) Module further captures long-range cross-temporal dependencies and strengthens spatial detail representation. In the decoding stage, the Cross-Space Interaction Enhancement Module employs cross-attention to improve feature discrimination and spatial consistency in change regions. Experiments on LEVIR-CD, GZ-CD, and SYSU-CD demonstrate that GLMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in CD accuracy, boundary preservation, and robustness to complex scenarios, while maintaining low parameter count and computational cost. These results validate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method.
author: Shengyan Liu, Chengye Zhu, Haoyu Yin, Kaibo Qin, Haifeng Lin, Junqing Huang, Min Xia, Liguo Weng

title: V-MTNet: A Hybrid Mamba-Transformer Network for Cloud Detection in Remote Sensing Images
content: Existing cloud detection methods struggle to simultaneously capture long-range dependencies and preserve fine-grained local details, limiting accuracy in complex scenes characterized by intricate terrain and diverse cloud formations. To overcome these challenges, we propose V-MTNet, a novel Visual Mamba-Transformer hybrid network for cloud detection in remote sensing images. V-MTNet employs a hierarchical encoder-decoder structure. The encoder integrates a Visual Mamba-Transformer mixer (V-MT), designed to leverage the efficient long-range modeling of Mamba and the superior local detail extraction of Transformer. The decoder integrates the features from the V-MT mixer and the multiscale fusion skip connections. These skip connections are enhanced by spatial-channel dual attention to bridge semantic gaps between the feature maps of the encoder and decoder and compensate for the loss of spatial detail in deep feature maps caused by downsampling. Comprehensive experiments conducted on multispectral remote sensing image datasets from Landsat-8, Sentinel-2, and GaoFen-1 satellites demonstrate that V-MTNet outperforms the state-of-the-art methods and achieves the best mIoU on three datasets: 0.9170, 0.9013, and 0.8910, respectively.
author: Xuan Cang, Wei Fang, Miaogen Ling

title: TuckerNet: A Multilayer Unsupervised Hyperspectral Change Detection Framework With Tensor Decomposition
content: Hyperspectral change detection plays a pivotal role in remote sensing by identifying subtle surface changes through the analysis of bitemporal hyperspectral images (HSIs). Existing unsupervised methods are often limited by sensitivity to pseudosample selection, complex hyperparameter tuning, reliance on handcrafted features, and loss of spectral–spatial coupling information. To overcome these limitations, we propose TuckerNet, a novel multilayer unsupervised change detection framework that leverages 3-D tensor representations to fully preserve the inherent spectral–spatial correlations in HSIs. At the core of our framework is Tucker convolution, a convolutional operation built on Tucker decomposition with adaptive rank estimation, enabling efficient extraction of low-dimensional spectral–spatial features without labeled data. In addition, a novel change detector integrates independent spectral discrepancy and local spatial analysis to achieve adaptive thresholding and generate accurate binary change maps. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that TuckerNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods, exhibiting strong robustness to hyperparameter variations and highly competitive computational efficiency.
author: Qingxi Wu, Jingyi Li, Nan Wang, Jiangtao Peng

title: Joint Classification of Hyperspectral and LiDAR Data Based on Dynamic Sparse-Dense Transformer and Mamba
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) provides rich spatial-spectral information, while light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data offers complementary elevation details. As a result, the fusion of HSI and LiDAR data to improve classification performance has emerged as a prominent research direction. Recently, Mamba has demonstrated significant advantages in long-range sequence modeling with linear computational complexity, whereas Transformer is particularly effective at capturing short-range dependencies. To fully harness the complementary strengths of both architectures, this article introduces a joint classification model for HSI and LiDAR data named dynamic sparse-Dense transformer and Mamba (DSDT-Mamba). Specifically, we design a dynamic sparse-dense transformer (DSDT) to extract intra-modal features from HSI and LiDAR data separately. The DSDT integrates sparse self-attention (SSA) and dense self-attention (DSA): SSA alleviates the negative impact of poor query-key matches on feature representation, while DSA preserves sufficient information flow throughout the network to promote discriminative feature learning. Furthermore, features from different modalities are concatenated and transformed into a long multimodal sequence, which is efficiently fused using Mamba to leverage its long-range dependency modeling capability. Experiments carried out on four datasets serve to confirm DSDT-Mamba, with results showing that DSDT-Mamba yields competitive or superior classification accuracy compared to other state-of-the-art approaches.
author: Zhe Meng, Zixin Yang, Feng Zhao

title: Critical Corrections of MODIS Over-Land Aerosol Optical Depth Data From 2016 to 2017 for Near Real Time Applications
content: Uncertainties from surface reflectance estimation, cloud screening, and aerosol model assumption constrain the near real time application of satellite aerosol data. This study develops a systematic quality control procedure (QC) that includes data preprocessing, empirical correction for lower boundary condition, regional microphysical bias, and negative data. It is applied to optimize the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) Collection 6.1 aerosol optical depth (AOD) product over land from June 2016 to May 2017, evaluated by ground-based aerosol robotic network (AERONET) data. The results show that, after QC, the coefficient of determination (r2) between MODIS-Aqua (Terra) and AERONET AOD increases from 0.69 (0.68) to 0.80 (0.81). The proportion of retrievals falling within the AERONET expected error range (0.05 ± 20%) rises by 18% (16%). At the same time, the root mean square error decreases from 0.138 (0.139) to 0.106 (0.100). The optimized dataset has improved performance across all regions and effectively captures the typical spatial distribution and seasonal variability of global aerosols over land. It highlights high-value regions, such as North Africa, East Asia, and Indian subcontinent, and reflects distinct seasonal phenomena including Saharan dust transport, East Asian dust storms, and biomass burning in the Southern Hemisphere. This dataset, maintaining near real time availability, can offer enhanced accuracy for evaluation and data assimilation of China Meteorological Administration’s global air quality model, as well as for studies on aerosol characteristics.
author: Xiaoyu Shen, Yufen Zhang, Jian Zhang, Zongru Yang, Chunhong Zhou

title: Multilevel Spatial Spectral Difference and Geometric Constraint Model for Airborne Snapshot Spectral Imaging
content: Hyperspectral remote sensing provides essential spectral information for fine-grained discrimination of surface materials and retrieval of ecological parameters. Snapshot hyperspectral imaging significantly enhances data acquisition efficiency; however, its reliance on compressed sampling and the susceptibility of airborne platforms to environmental perturbations often result in severe spatial–spectral degradation, imposing stricter constraints on reconstruction algorithms. To overcome these challenges, we propose a multilevel spatial–spectral differential joint-attention model. By performing differential operations on both global self-attention and neighborhood attention, the model suppresses common-mode background interference and enhances discriminative spatial features, thereby enabling collaborative modeling of long-range spatial dependencies and local details. Moreover, incorporating elevation–geometry constraints into the loss function effectively mitigates spectral variability and geometric distortions induced by terrain undulations. Multiscene experiments demonstrate notable improvements: a spectral angle mapper (SAM) of 0.0442 in the Houston scene ($\downarrow$ 4.24%), a peak signal-to-noise ratio of 41.12 dB in the MUESLI scene ($\uparrow$ 0.28%), and a relative average spectral error (RASE) of 284.75 in the MDAS scene ($\downarrow$ 3.67%). When elevation data are integrated, SAM in the Houston scene is further reduced by 0.68%, and RASE in the MDAS scene decreases by 1.71%. The model achieves an inference latency of 85.7 ms ($\downarrow$ 38%), striking a favorable balance between reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency. Extensive validations on the U.S. National Ecological Observation Network ecological monitoring data further confirm the model's cross-site and multiplatform (UAV–satellite) transferability, enabling high-precision estimation of forest structural parameters, carbon-stock assessment, and ecological monitoring.
author: Mingda Hao, Yibo Wang, Peijun Du, Jinnian Wang

title: GSFFE-Net: Global Semantic Feature Fusion Enhancement Network for Multiclass Multitarget Detection in SAR Images
content: Multiclass multitarget detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery aims to simultaneously, rapidly, and efficiently detect and recognize multiple targets of interest within SAR images. It plays a crucial role in both military and civilian applications. However, most existing SAR target detection algorithms are designed for single target classes. When applied to multiclass detection scenarios, they often require multiple passes over the same scene using a series of single-class models, which results in the poor performance, high computational costs, and long processing times. The development of algorithms capable of detecting multiple target classes simultaneously can effectively address these challenges. Nevertheless, compared to single-class detection methods, multiclass detection algorithms must account for more complex interclass similarities and intraclass variations. In addition, challenges, such as multiscale variations caused by diverse target sizes, background clutter, and terrain interference, become more pronounced in multiclass detection scenarios. In response to the above issues, this article proposes a novel global semantic feature fusion enhancement network (GSFFE-Net) to address multiclass SAR target detection with enhanced global context modeling. The proposed framework integrates three key modules: first, wavelet feature recombination module (WFRM), which decomposes SAR images into low-frequency background semantic features and high-frequency target details via 2-D wavelet transform, then recombines multichannel features to exploit prior spatial-frequency information. Second, global semantic attention spatial pyramid pooling fast module, which employs dual global pooling branches to capture global context, combined with a multiscale convolutional attention mechanism to enhance cross-channel feature synergy. Third, CMUNeXt block, inspired by medical image segmentation, which uses large-kernel depthwise separable convolutions and inverted residual structures to model long-range spatial dependencies and retain low-level features. Extensive experiments on the large-scale SARDet-100 K dataset demonstrate that GSFFE-Net achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, reaching 89.0$\%$, 71.8$\%$, and 64.3$\%$ for mAP@50, mAP@75, and mAP@50:95, respectively, surpassing the baseline by 5.2$\%$, 11.9$\%$, and 9.7$\%$. Ablation studies confirm the contributions of the proposed modules. This work provides a robust solution for multiclass SAR target detection in complex scenarios, while achieving a high inference speed of 769 FPS with only 3.9M parameters, demonstrating strong potential for real-world deployment.
author: Zekang Fan, Lingjun Zhao, Kui Liu, Kefeng Ji, Gangyao Kuang

title: A UAV-based framework for field-scale extraction of leaf inclination angles in soybean canopies
content:
Publication date: July 2026

Source: Remote Sensing of Environment, Volume 340

Author(s): Qing Li, Dalei Hao, Yelu Zeng

title: The Smart Advantage: How Artificial Intelligence Is Transforming Inspection And Metrology In Semiconductor Manufacturing
content:
There is no doubt that the semiconductor industry is in an era of rapid and profound transformation, driven by an increasing demand for smaller, faster, and more powerful chips. As the speed of innovation continues to advance, so does the pressure on semiconductor manufacturers to detect and address defects and inconsistencies with near-perfect accuracy to keep pace with this demand.

Manual inspection methods, which have traditionally been relied upon throughout the field, struggle to evolve and scale in step with production volumes and complexity, limiting the ability to boost production speed and output. As a result, Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly becoming an essential tool in inspection and metrology, offering ways to streamline operations, increase accuracy and therefore yield, and keep up with the scale and complexity of modern chip design.

Sharper standards: How AI is elevating defect detection

AI is transforming semiconductor inspection and metrology by introducing unprecedented automation, speed, and adaptability to defect detection processes. AI-driven systems tap into the power of Big Data to uncover patterns and anomalies that traditional methods often miss. Indeed, in most cases, AI can make better decisions than human operators, with fewer false rejections, and provide a more complex, enhanced analysis than traditional algorithms, which tend to rely on simple boundaries and a binary pass/fail system. As well as minimizing wastage of energy and materials, AI analysis can, in most cases, also run faster than standard algorithms, resulting in additional time savings – all adding up to a significant boost to the bottom line for semiconductor manufacturers.

While these benefits are considerable in their own right, interestingly it’s not necessarily the primary reason that we see businesses seeking out AI-integrated platforms. In my experience at Nordson, what truly drives interest is AI’s unique ability to solve challenges that were previously not addressable. When working with the most minute of parts, traditional methods typically struggle to detect certain microscopic flaws or anomalies, for example in corner fill inspection where conventional methods like blob analysis come up short. Through deep learning, however, AI-integrated systems like our SQ3000 Multi-Function System can detect and flag issues that could otherwise be misread or missed entirely – with unparallelled speed and efficiency, and without reliance on skilled labor.

What’s more, AI-powered systems grow smarter with each inspection cycle and – as the wealth of source data and input evolves over time – edge closer to the fully autonomous, self-optimizing inspection systems that we expect will define our industry’s future. This evolution is exemplified, for instance, in the inspection of TSVs, evaluated at a micron level. Whereas conventional methods require around an hour to complete this intricate process, generative AI unlocks almost a hundredfold improvement, enabling us to achieve the same level of accuracy in under a minute.

Another of the biggest game-changers in the field is AI’s capacity for real-time, in-line inspection. Where heavy data analysis would previously have slowed the production line, AI now enables rapid, high-volume data processing without compromising speed. As systems evolve, we’re also seeing huge leaps in how this data can be leveraged, with machine learning (ML) models that automatically adjust quickly to new production requirements without reprogramming. On a fast-paced production floor, this adaptability can be critical, allowing semiconductor manufacturers to reduce bottlenecks and boost productivity.

AI unplugged: The self-teaching systems transforming inspection

Critical to any discussion around AI is the advancement of ML, revolutionizing inspection by creating AI systems that become smarter and more adaptable with each interaction. Whereas supervised learning relies on pre-labeled data examples to train AI models to recognize specific types of defects, unsupervised learning is perhaps an even more exciting area to watch. This approach to ML doesn’t require labeled data input and simply analyzes the data to identify patterns, outliers, or anomalies without prior guidance, meaning it is able to detect unknown and novel defects that haven’t been seen before, or that customers may not even know exist. Here at Nordson, both supervised and unsupervised learning play pivotal roles in our AI ecosystem, Eagle AI, driving ML advancements from all angles – and we are also seeing advances in automatic labeling showing real future promise.

Advanced ML, however, comes hand in hand with its own set of challenges. Today, one of the foremost barriers to truly harnessing the power of AI arguably lies in managing customer data to train these intelligent systems. Security and confidentiality are top priorities for our customers, and many are understandably reluctant to provide direct access to their data. Whilst we’ve successfully developed advanced, secure solutions to address these concerns – including private cloud domains and protected remote access – access to usable, real-world data is critical to future ML. As such, as in any industry, it will continue to be a focus for us to expand these secure, scalable solutions and help build trust in AI and in smart solutions.

A bright future for AI-driven inspection

Within the inspection and metrology landscape, it’s evident that AI advancements are not just significant but completely transformative, addressing key challenges and delivering benefits from process optimization to cost savings. For our team here at Nordson, we believe so wholeheartedly in its groundbreaking potential for our industry that we dedicate a huge proportion of our resources to exploring emerging growth areas like predictive maintenance, generative AI, and automated ML. The other part is focused on deploying and refining our current solutions portfolio, including ongoing systems development and supervised learning. In doing so, we can continue to serve customer demand and deepen our data insights, while also shifting the dial of what AI can achieve in the long term for the industry. With the momentum of the AI evolution showing no signs of slowing down, its reach seems poised to expand exponentially, unlocking powerful new applications and enhancing our ability to serve our customers, delivering more intelligent solutions, superior service and a brighter, smarter era for semiconductor manufacturing.

The post The Smart Advantage: How Artificial Intelligence Is Transforming Inspection And Metrology In Semiconductor Manufacturing appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.
author: Charlie Zhu

title: Heterogeneous NPU Data Movement: What The Execution Flow Shows
content:
Heterogeneous NPU designs bring together multiple specialized compute engines to support the range of operators required by modern AI models. This approach enables coverage across diverse workloads, but it also introduces a structural consequence: intermediate data must move between those engines. That movement consumes power, adds latency, and requires additional silicon resources, with effects that grow alongside model complexity.

A 2024 presentation from Intel describing its Gen 4 NPU architecture provides a detailed view into how this plays out in practice. The material walks through the execution of a transformer graph step by step, making the underlying data flow explicit. In doing so, it offers a concrete example of how architectural boundaries influence system behavior.

The architecture: Two compute domains

The Gen 4 NPU is organized around two distinct types of compute engines. Matrix operations are handled by MAC arrays, which are optimized for dense linear algebra and form the core of transformer inference. Alongside them sit SHAVE DSPs, which provide general-purpose vector processing for operations that fall outside the capabilities of fixed-function matrix units.

These engines are connected through shared infrastructure, including scratchpad memory, cache, an MMU, and DMA. This structure allows the system to support a broad set of operators, but it also creates clear boundaries between compute domains. Whenever execution shifts from one type of engine to another, intermediate data must pass through this shared memory system.

Fig. 1: Intel Gen 4 NPU block diagram from the 2024 Tech Tour. Six Neural Compute Engines, each pairing a MAC Array with SHAVE DSPs, sit beneath shared MMU, DMA, and Scratchpad RAM infrastructure.

Multi-head attention as a case study

The implications of this design become more apparent when examining Multi-Head Attention, a central operation in transformer models. Its formulation is straightforward, combining matrix multiplications with a SoftMax normalization step to produce attention-weighted outputs. In practice, however, executing this sequence requires multiple distinct operations, each of which must be mapped onto available compute resources.

The process begins with linear projections to generate query, key, and value vectors, followed by a matrix multiplication that computes similarity scores. These scores are then normalized using SoftMax before a second matrix multiplication applies the resulting attention weights. The sequence concludes with concatenation and a final projection. While each step is well understood in isolation, the transitions between them reveal how data moves through the system.

Fig. 2: Intel’s MHA computation graph, showing the full operator chain: three Linear projections, two MatMuls, SoftMax, Concat, and a final Linear.

Following the data

In the execution flow described in the presentation, the first matrix multiplication runs on the MAC arrays, producing intermediate activations within that subsystem. The next step, SoftMax, cannot be executed on the same hardware, which requires the data to move out of the MAC array domain, traverse the shared memory hierarchy, and arrive at the SHAVE DSPs for processing.

Once SoftMax completes, the resulting data must again move through shared memory, this time returning to the MAC arrays to perform the second matrix multiplication. The pattern that emerges is a repeated transfer of activations between compute domains, with shared memory acting as the intermediary.

This sequence—MAC arrays to DSPs and back again—occurs within a single attention block. When repeated across multiple heads and layers in a transformer model, it becomes a recurring aspect of execution rather than an isolated event.

Fig. 3: Step 1: The first MatMul (word vector similarity dot-product) executes on the MAC Arrays. Intermediate activations are produced inside the MAC Array subsystem.

System-level implications

The effects of this data movement extend beyond the transfers themselves. Each exchange of intermediate data consumes memory bandwidth, as values must be written out and read back in. This activity places sustained demand on the memory subsystem, even though it does not directly contribute to new computation.

At the same time, coordination between engines introduces additional overhead. Control logic and DMA mechanisms are required to manage data transfers and ensure that operations occur in the correct sequence. These elements occupy silicon area and consume power as part of the system’s normal operation.

Execution timing is also affected. Because each stage depends on the completion of the previous one and the availability of data in shared memory, transitions between engines can introduce idle periods. As models grow deeper and incorporate more complex attention variants, these effects accumulate, making data movement an increasingly significant component of overall execution.

Fig. 4: Step 2: SoftMax (similarity normalization) executes on the SHAVE DSPs. Intermediate activations have crossed from the MAC Array subsystem into DSP territory via shared memory.

An alternative approach

A different architectural approach is to use a unified, programmable compute fabric capable of executing multiple operator types within the same subsystem. In this model, matrix operations, normalization functions, and other elements of the workload are handled without requiring data to move between distinct engine types.

By keeping intermediate activations within a single compute domain, this approach reduces reliance on shared memory for inter-stage communication. It also changes the role of control logic and data movement, shifting more of the system’s activity toward computation rather than coordination. As model architectures evolve, programmability allows new operator patterns to be supported without introducing additional execution boundaries.

Architectural tradeoffs

The contrast between these approaches reflects a broader architectural tradeoff. Heterogeneous designs rely on specialized engines and explicit data movement to handle different parts of a workload, while unified designs emphasize flexibility within a single programmable fabric.

Each approach has implications for how data flows through the system, how resources are allocated, and how performance scales with model complexity. The differences become most visible in workloads like transformer inference, where multiple operator types are tightly coupled within a single execution graph.

Fig. 5: Step 3: The second MatMul (attention score calculation) executes on the MAC Arrays. Activations have completed a full round-trip: MAC Arrays → SHAVE DSPs → MAC Arrays.

Conclusion

The execution flow of transformer models provides a clear lens through which to examine NPU architecture. In heterogeneous designs, movement of intermediate data between compute engines is a recurring and necessary part of operation, with measurable effects on power, latency, and system utilization.

For SoC designers, the key consideration is not simply how individual operators are accelerated, but how the system handles the data between them. Architectural choices made at that level shape the overall efficiency of the processor as models continue to evolve.

Source: Intel Tech Tour 2024 presentation, “Transformer Architecture on Intel’s NPU / Multi-Head Attention Flowchart.” All diagrams reproduced from public presentation for editorial commentary purposes.

The post Heterogeneous NPU Data Movement: What The Execution Flow Shows appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.
author: Steve Roddy

title: Research Bits: Apr. 14
content:
Authentication for edge devices

Researchers from the University of Hong Kong, Tsinghua University, and the Southern University of Science and Technology designed a privacy-preserving system for edge devices that combines physically unclonable functions and compute-in-memory.

The Co-Located Authentication and Processing (CLAP) system integrates authentication and processing functions within a unified memristor-based platform that leverages the device’s inherent physical randomness to provide a unique security identifier for device authentication. At the same time, the memristor’s compute-in-memory capability enables efficient data analysis.

“We exploit both characteristics simultaneously. Current solutions separate security from analysis modules and memory from computation units, creating significant hardware and energy overheads—prohibitive for resource-limited edge applications. Our hardware-level integration maintains authentication reliability and computational accuracy without traditional inefficiencies,” said Zhengwu Liu, a research assistant professor from the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at HKU, in a statement.

A proof-of-concept demonstrated secure electrocardiogram (ECG) data collection, achieving device authentication with an area under the curve of 99.46%, along with efficient signal compression and area reduction compared to conventional implementations. [1]

High-temperature memristor

Researchers from the University of Southern California, Airforce Research Lab, Kumamoto University, and TetraMem built a high-temperature memristor that kept working reliably at 700 degrees Celsius, with the possibility of reaching even higher temperatures.

The memristor was constructed of tungsten for the top layer, hafnium oxide ceramic in the middle, and graphene on the bottom. The device survived more than one billion switching cycles and held data for over 50 hours at 700 degrees. It also ran on 1.5 volts with an operation speed of tens of nanoseconds.

In addition to extremely high temperature environments and space exploration, the team says the memristor is ideal for efficient matrix multiplication in AI systems. [2]

DNA plus perovskites

Researchers from Penn State and the University of Cincinnati combined synthetic DNA with crystalline perovskite to create a memristor storage device that consumes much less power while having a higher storage capacity than traditional flash drives.

“Biology and electronics are different domains,” said Kavya S. Keremane, co-corresponding author and postdoctoral researcher in materials science and engineering at Penn State, in a press release. “Bridging these two fields required developing an entirely new materials platform that allows them to function seamlessly together. By combining the information storage capabilities of DNA with the exceptional electronic properties of perovskite semiconductors, we created a bio-hybrid system that fundamentally changes how low-power memory devices can be designed.”

The device uses customized DNA sequences to provide structural order, tunable electrical conductivity, and functional control. “We can computationally determine exactly which sequences we need and how long they should be, and then we can rationally design them with synthetic DNA,” said Neela H. Yennawar, research professor and director of the Penn State Huck Institutes of the Life Sciences’ Biomolecular Interactions Core Facility, in a statement. “These structures can be systematically doped with silver and other ions and engineered to interface seamlessly with perovskites — transforming DNA from a biological macromolecule into a programmable, multifunctional nanomaterials platform.”

Electrons reliably moved through the device when less than 0.1 volt was applied, and responded when the current was switched. The device consistently performed up to almost 250 degrees Fahrenheit and at room temperature for more than six weeks. [3]

References

[1] Z. Liu, Z. Wang, C. Ding, et al. Privacy-preserving data analysis using a memristor chip with colocated authentication and processing. Sci. Adv. 12, eady5485 (2026). https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.ady5485

[2] J. Zhao, C. S. Jorgensen, K. Mahalingam, et al. High-temperature memristors enabled by interfacial engineering. Science 0, eaeb9934 https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aeb9934

[3] K. S. Keremane, A. Gorthy, L. Zheng, et al. “Molecularly Engineered Highly Stable Memristors with Ultra-Low Operational Voltage: Integrating Synthetic DNA with Quasi-2D Perovskites.” Advanced Functional Materials (2026): e30539. https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.202530539
