Transcriptomics-based biomarkers derived from bulk and single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) offer a promising avenue for capturing tumour-immune interactions, yet the cross-cohort generalisability of existing prediction models remains unclear.We systematically benchmark nine state-of-the-art transcriptomic ICI response predictors, five bulk RNA-seq-based models (COMPASS, IRNet, NetBio, IKCScore, and TNBC-ICI) and four scRNA-seq-based models (PRECISE, DeepGeneX, Tres and scCURE), using publicly available independent datasets unseen during model development. Overall, predictive performance was modest: bulk RNA-seq models performed at or near chance level across most cohorts, while scRNA-seq models showed only marginal improvements. Pathway-level analyses revealed sparse and inconsistent biomarker signals across models. Although scRNA-seq-based predictors converged on immune-related programs such as allograft rejection, bulk RNA-seq-based models exhibited little reproducible overlap. PRECISE and NetBio identified the most coherent immune-related themes, whereas IRNet predominantly captured metabolic pathways weakly aligned with ICI biology. Together, these findings demonstrate the limited cross-cohort robustness and biological consistency of current transcriptomic ICI prediction models, underscoring the need for improved domain adaptation, standardised preprocessing, and biologically grounded model design.
author: Yuheng Liang, Lucy Chhuo, Ahmadreza Argha, Nona Farbehi, Lu Chen, Roohallah Alizadehsani, Mehdi Hosseinzadeh, Amin Beheshti, Thantrira Porntaveetusm, Youqiong Ye, Hamid Alinejad-Rokny

title: Large Language Model Post-Training: A Unified View of Off-Policy and On-Policy Learning
content:
arXiv:2604.07941v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Post-training has become central to turning pretrained large language models (LLMs) into aligned, capable, and deployable systems. Recent progress spans supervised fine-tuning (SFT), preference optimization, reinforcement learning (RL), process supervision, verifier-guided methods, distillation, and multi-stage pipelines. Yet these methods are often discussed in fragmented ways, organized by labels or objectives rather than by the behavioral bottlenecks they address. This survey argues that LLM post-training is best understood as structured intervention on model behavior. We organize the field first by trajectory provenance, which defines two primary regimes: off-policy learning on externally supplied trajectories and on-policy learning on learner-generated rollouts. We then interpret methods through two recurring roles -- effective support expansion, which makes useful behaviors more reachable, and policy reshaping, which improves behavior within already reachable regions -- together with a complementary systems-level role, behavioral consolidation, which preserves, transfers, and amortizes useful behavior across stages and model transitions. Under this view, SFT may serve either support expansion or policy reshaping; preference optimization is usually off-policy reshaping, though online variants move closer to learner-generated states. On-policy RL often improves behavior on learner-generated states, but stronger guidance can also make hard-to-reach reasoning paths reachable. Distillation is often better understood as consolidation rather than only compression, and hybrid pipelines emerge as coordinated multi-stage compositions. Overall, the framework helps diagnose post-training bottlenecks and reason about stage composition, suggesting that progress increasingly depends on coordinated systems design rather than any single dominant objective.
author: Shiwan Zhao, Zhihu Wang, Xuyang Zhao, Jiaming Zhou, Caiyue Xu, Chenfei Liu, Liting Zhang, Yuhang Jia, Yanzhe Zhang, Hualong Yu, Zichen Xu, Qicheng Li, Yong Qin

title: Reproduction Beyond Benchmarks: ConstBERT and ColBERT-v2 Across Backends and Query Distributions
content:
arXiv:2604.09982v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Reproducibility must validate architectural robustness, not just numerical accuracy. We evaluate ColBERT-v2 and ConstBERT across five dimensions, finding that while ConstBERT reproduces within 0.05% MRR@10 on MS-MARCO, both models show a drop of 86-97% on long, narrative queries (TREC ToT 2025). Ablations prove this failure is architectural: performance plateaus at 20 words because the MaxSim operator's uniform token weighting cannot distinguish signal from filler noise. Furthermore, undocumented backend parameters create an 8-point gap due to ConstBERT's sparse centroid coverage, and fine-tuning with 3x more data actually degrades performance by up to 29%. We conclude that architectural constraints in multi-vector retrieval cannot be overcome by adaptation alone. Code: https://github.com/utshabkg/multi-vector-reproducibility.
author: Utshab Kumar Ghosh, Ashish David, Shubham Chatterjee

title: A Queueing-Theoretic Framework for Dynamic Attack Surfaces: Data-Integrated Risk Analysis and Adaptive Defense
content:
arXiv:2604.10427v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We develop a queueing-theoretic framework to model the temporal evolution of cyber-attack surfaces, where the number of active vulnerabilities is represented as the backlog of a queue. Vulnerabilities arrive as they are discovered or created, and leave the system when they are patched or successfully exploited. Building on this model, we study how automation affects attack and defense dynamics by introducing an AI amplification factor that scales arrival, exploit, and patching rates. Our analysis shows that even symmetric automation can increase the rate of successful exploits. We validate the model using vulnerability data collected from an open source software supply chain and show that it closely matches real-world attack surface dynamics. Empirical results reveal heavy-tailed patching times, which we prove induce long-range dependence in vulnerability backlog and help explain persistent cyber risk. Utilizing our queueing abstraction for the attack surface, we develop a systematic approach for cyber risk mitigation. We formulate the dynamic defense problem as a constrained Markov decision process with resource-budget and switching-cost constraints, and develop a reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm that achieves provably near-optimal regret. Numerical experiments validate the approach and demonstrate that our adaptive RL-based defense policies significantly reduce successful exploits and mitigate heavy-tail queue events. Using trace-driven experiments on the ARVO dataset, we show that the proposed RL-based defense policy reduces the average number of active vulnerabilities in a software supply chain by over 90% compared to existing defense practices, without increasing the overall maintenance budget. Our results allow defenders to quantify cumulative exposure risk under long-range dependent attack dynamics and to design adaptive defense strategies with provable efficiency.
author: Jihyeon Yun, Abdullah Yasin Etcibasi, Ming Shi, C. Emre Koksal

title: Revisiting Compositionality in Dual-Encoder Vision-Language Models: The Role of Inference
content:
arXiv:2604.11496v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Dual-encoder Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP are often characterized as bag-of-words systems due to their poor performance on compositional benchmarks. We argue that this limitation may stem less from deficient representations than from the standard inference protocol based on global cosine similarity. First, through controlled diagnostic experiments, we show that explicitly enforcing fine-grained region-segment alignment at inference dramatically improves compositional performance without updating pretrained encoders. We then introduce a lightweight transformer that learns such alignments directly from frozen patch and token embeddings. Comparing against full fine-tuning and prior end-to-end compositional training methods, we find that although these approaches improve in-domain retrieval, their gains do not consistently transfer under distribution shift. In contrast, learning localized alignment over frozen representations matches full fine-tuning on in-domain retrieval while yielding substantial improvements on controlled out-of-domain compositional benchmarks. These results identify global embedding matching as a key bottleneck in dual-encoder VLMs and highlight the importance of alignment mechanisms for robust compositional generalization.
author: Imanol Miranda, Ander Salaberria, Eneko Agirre, Gorka Azkune

title: The Spectrascapes Dataset: Street-view imagery beyond the visible captured using a mobile platform
content:
arXiv:2604.13315v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: High-resolution data in spatial and temporal contexts is imperative for developing climate resilient cities. Current datasets for monitoring urban parameters are developed primarily using manual inspections, embedded-sensing, remote sensing, or standard street-view imagery (RGB). These methods and datasets are often constrained respectively by poor scalability, inconsistent spatio-temporal resolutions, overhead views or low spectral information. We present a novel method and its open implementation: a multi-spectral terrestrial-view dataset that circumvents these limitations. This dataset consists of 17,718 street level multi-spectral images captured with RGB, Near-infrared, and Thermal imaging sensors on bikes, across diverse urban morphologies (village, town, small city, and big urban area) in the Netherlands. Strict emphasis is put on data calibration and quality while also providing the details of our data collection methodology (including the hardware and software details). To the best of our knowledge, Spectrascapes is the first open-access dataset of its kind. Finally, we demonstrate two downstream use-cases enabled using this dataset and provide potential research directions in the machine learning, urban planning and remote sensing domains.
author: Akshit Gupta, Joris Timmermans, Filip Biljecki, Remko Uijlenhoet

title: Functional Emotions or Situational Contexts? A Discriminating Test from the Mythos Preview System Card
content:
arXiv:2604.13466v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: The Claude Mythos Preview system card deploys emotion vectors, sparse autoencoder (SAE) features, and activation verbalisers to study model internals during misaligned behaviour. The two primary toolkits are not jointly reported on the most alignment-relevant episodes. This note identifies two hypotheses that are qualitatively consistent with the published results: that the emotion vectors track functional emotions that causally drive behaviour, or that they are a projection of a richer situational-context structure onto human emotional axes. The hypotheses can be distinguished by cross-referencing the two toolkits on episodes where only one is currently reported: most directly, applying emotion probes to the strategic concealment episodes analysed only with SAE features. If emotion probes show flat activation while SAE features are strongly active, the alignment-relevant structure lies outside the emotion subspace. Which hypothesis is correct determines whether emotion-based monitoring will robustly detect dangerous model behaviour or systematically miss it.
author: Hiranya V. Peiris

title: From Feelings to Metrics: Understanding and Formalizing How Users Vibe-Test LLMs
content:
arXiv:2604.14137v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Evaluating LLMs is challenging, as benchmark scores often fail to capture models' real-world usefulness. Instead, users often rely on ``vibe-testing'': informal experience-based evaluation, such as comparing models on coding tasks related to their own workflow. While prevalent, vibe-testing is often too ad hoc and unstructured to analyze or reproduce at scale. In this work, we study how vibe-testing works in practice and then formalize it to support systematic analysis. We first analyze two empirical resources: (1) a survey of user evaluation practices, and (2) a collection of in-the-wild model comparison reports from blogs and social media. Based on these resources, we formalize vibe-testing as a two-part process: users personalize both what they test and how they judge responses. We then introduce a proof-of-concept evaluation pipeline that follows this formulation by generating personalized prompts and comparing model outputs using user-aware subjective criteria. In experiments on coding benchmarks, we find that combining personalized prompts and user-aware evaluation can change which model is preferred, reflecting the role of vibe-testing in practice. These findings suggest that formalized vibe-testing can serve as a useful approach for bridging benchmark scores and real-world experience.
author: Itay Itzhak, Eliya Habba, Gabriel Stanovsky, Yonatan Belinkov

title: What It Actually Takes to Run Code on 200M€ Supercomputer
content:
Inside MareNostrum V: SLURM schedulers, fat-tree topologies, and scaling pipelines across 8,000 nodes in a 19th-century chapel

title: Building My Own Personal AI Assistant: A Chronicle, Part 2
content:
Building a personal AI assistant is rarely a single, monolithic effort. In this piece, I walk through my latest addition: a task breaker module that decomposes complex goals into structured, actionable steps — and why that single component changed how I think about AI-driven productivity.

The post Building My Own Personal AI Assistant: A Chronicle, Part 2 appeared first on Towards Data Science.
author: Ivo Bernardo

The problem with agent memory today

The post memweave: Zero-Infra AI Agent Memory with Markdown and SQLite — No Vector Database Required appeared first on Towards Data Science.
author: Sachin Sharma

title: Introduction to Deep Evidential Regression for Uncertainty Quantification
content:
Machine learning models can be confident even when they shouldn't be. This article introduces Deep Evidential Regression (DER), a method that lets neural networks rapidly express what they don't know.

The post Introduction to Deep Evidential Regression for Uncertainty Quantification appeared first on Towards Data Science.
author: Benjamin Li

title: Efficient Inversion of Multiple Magnetic Dipole Models Based on Deep Learning Prior Analysis and Penalty Function Optimization
content: The inversion of multiple magnetic dipoles plays a critical role in applications such as geological exploration and spacecraft magnetic modeling. The primary challenges include an unknown number of magnetic sources, high sensitivity to noise, and low inversion accuracy. In this article, we propose a novel 3-D inversion method for multiple magnetic dipole models that integrates deep-learning-based prior analysis with a penalty function optimization strategy. A lightweight convolutional attention network, termed magnetic-gradient-tensor-invariant net (MGTI-Net), is developed to extract prior information from the invariants of the magnetic gradient tensor, thereby enabling accurate identification of the dipole count and providing preliminary estimates of their positions. By incorporating a penalty function into an improved least squares estimation framework, the proposed method robustly inverts the spatial positions and magnetic moments of the dipoles. Simulation results demonstrate that MGTI-Net can reliably determine the number of dipoles in a 3-D space and maintain a classification accuracy above 75% even under 50% relative random noise interference. Moreover, the improved least squares estimation can constrain the position and magnetic moment errors within 0.01 m and 0.1 A  $\cdot $  m2, respectively, under 15% relative noise, reducing the error by more than one order of magnitude compared with conventional least squares methods. Experimental findings corroborate the simulation analysis, confirming that the new approach can accurately recover magnetic source parameters.
author: Yiding Wang, Shengxin Lin, Donghua Pan, Chongyu Jin, Yitao Chen, Yuxiao Zhang, Liyi Li

title: Precise Sensor Orientation of High-Resolution Agile Optical Satellites With Star–Earth and Moon–Earth Collaborative Observations
content: Benefitting from the enhanced maneuverable imaging capability, an agile optical satellite could collect not only Earth images but also star images and Moon images, which provides a novel star–Earth and Moon–Earth collaborative observation mode. In this mode, the stars or lunar surface points could be considered as accurate control points to geometrically calibrate camera installation parameters (CIPs) in time. Aiming at such a novel mode, a feasible, precise sensor orientation method for high-resolution agile optical satellites with the star–Earth and Moon–Earth collaborative observations was presented. In the presented method, the CIPs were first calibrated with star images or Moon images, and then used to perform the sensor orientation of Earth images. The LuoJia3-02 panchromatic linear-array images were tested. The experimental results showed that the sensor orientation accuracy achieved by the star–Earth collaborative observation and the Moon–Earth collaborative observation was, respectively, 15.055 pixels and 13.692 pixels, which was almost the same as that in the conventional Earth observation. It demonstrates that the presented method could get rid of the high dependence on ground calibration fields in the conventional external geometric calibration. Moreover, it could calibrate the CIPs at a very high frequency (e.g., in each satellite orbit) and thereby improve the sensor orientation accuracy of optical satellite images.
author: Jinshan Cao, Jianping Zhao, Yingdong Pi, Mi Wang

title: Theoretical Modeling of Sea-State Bias in Interferometric Wide-Swath Altimetry
content: Sea-state bias (SSB) is an important source of error in nadir altimetry. Here, we focus on understanding from first principles how SSB will affect measurements over the ocean from the new interferometric swath altimeter KaRIn, on-board the Surface Water Ocean Topography (SWOT) mission. We develop a complete analytical model of SSB for KaRIn that explicitly accounts for the ocean surface geometry and motion, the acquisition geometry, instrument characteristics, and processing effects. Our modeling of the surface relies on a two-scale approach, where relevant properties of the surface waves resulting from their nonlinear interactions are encoded in both the joint distribution of surface elevation, slopes, and vertical velocity for the long waves as well as statistical moments for the short waves. To account for the so-called hydrodynamic modulation of short waves by longer waves, the latter moments are expressed as functions of the long-wave elevation. In contrast to previous approaches, we resort to a first-order Lagrangian weakly nonlinear formalism to compute these wave statistics, which leads to fully tractable expressions. This derivation of the surface properties resulting from the hydrodynamic modulation is in itself an important result of this work, with potential applications beyond the scope of this study. Our SSB model is a first step, which allows us to investigate, for the first time, the type of SSB dependence on sea state and on across-track distance that can be expected for KaRIn.
author: Samuel Osina, Frédéric Nouguier, Alejandro Bohé, Louis Marié, Bertrand Chapron, Fabrice Ardhuin

title: Deep Learning-Based Rain Rate Classification Using 5G Cellular Network Signals
content: In recent years, the frequency and intensity of extreme rainfall events have underscored the need for innovative technologies that provide rapid and accurate meteorological measurements. Given the high spatial and temporal variability of rainfall, there is an urgent demand for real-time monitoring methods with adequate spatiotemporal resolution. This study proposes a novel deep learning-based classifier model to estimate rainfall based on the signal variation of the 5G cellular network received by mobile phone terminals. The proposed method includes four essential characteristics: 1) employing a 5G mobile phone to collect reference signal strength with the variation of rainfall; 2) recording data at one second intervals using a 50 m short distance as the signal transmission path; 3) meteorological variables were integrated to enhance classification accuracy across varying rainfall rates; and 4) optimizing the classifier model with the Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA). The results showed that the binary classification task achieved an overall accuracy of 99.42% with a 0.1 mm/min threshold. The multiclass classification accuracy of the five-level rainfall intensity classification reached 98.54%, which is at least 2% higher than other studies in terms of accuracy. As a result, combining signal data with meteorological variables improved classification performance, where atmospheric pressure exhibited the most significant impact, followed by air humidity and temperature. This approach proposes a cost-effective solution for capturing uneven rainfall dynamics, particularly in remote areas lacking traditional meteorological stations. By enhancing the spatiotemporal resolution of rainfall data, the proposed method supports the development of robust weather prediction systems, achieving wide area refinement and fine-grained monitoring and bridging gaps in existing systems.
author: Yanning Mao, Jian Wang, Fiallos Manuel, Soon-Thiam Khu

title: A Geostationary Satellite Precipitation Estimation Machine Learning Model With Dynamic Sample Balancing for Fengyun-4B
content: High-precision, real-time precipitation estimation is critical for improving the accuracy of meteorological forecasting and disaster warnings. However, mainstream satellite remote sensing inversion techniques are often limited by their reliance on cloud-top information. In addition, estimation accuracy is further constrained by the severe category imbalance in precipitation samples and the widespread use of low-spatial-resolution reanalysis data, making it difficult for existing products to meet the practical application requirements. To address these challenges, this study proposes a high-precision real-time precipitation inversion algorithm named geostationary satellite precipitation estimation—dynamic updating machine learning (GSPE-DML), which is based on the Fengyun-4B (FY-4B) satellite and machine learning. The algorithm is designed with two core innovations. First, it incorporates high-resolution (3 km) CMA-BJ v2.0 reanalysis data to replace conventional low-resolution datasets such as ERA5, thereby capturing more detailed information on the subcloud vertical atmospheric structure. Second, a real-time dynamic sample balance mechanism is introduced, which adaptively adjusts category weights during model training to effectively mitigate the precipitation sample imbalance problem. A comparative analysis conducted across different temporal scales indicates that the performance of GSPE-DML is better than that of real-time precipitation products such as FY-4B quantitative precipitation estimation (FY4B-QPE), APCP, and Global Satellite Mapping of Precipitation (GSMAP)-NRT across several key metrics. On the hourly scale, in particular, GSPE-DML maintained a Heidke skill score (HSS) above 0.52 and achieved a correlation coefficient (CC) of 0.45 with ground observations. Notably, its false alarm rate (FAR) was significantly lower than that of the GSMAP-NRT product across all four quarters. A case study of Typhoon “Doksuri” further validated the robustness of the algorithm. The inversion results showed minimal deviation (−0.01) from ground observations and high consistency in spatial patterns, successfully reconstructing the intense precipitation band near the typhoon’s eyewall and the weak precipitation structure within its outer spiral rainbands.
author: Hanna Sai, Yanhui Xie, Husi Letu, Mengyuan Cui, Shanhu Bao, Na Xu, Xiaoqing Li, Jinbiao Zhu, Wen Qiu, Jiancheng Shi, Dabin Ji

title: Slant Visual Range Retrieval and Analysis With Multisource Satellite Observations and SBDART Model
content: Atmospheric visibility assessment from space remains challenging due to the complex aerosol-radiation interactions and observation geometry effects. This study presents a methodology for retrieving slant visual range (SVR) by using multisource remote sensing datasets integrated with radiative transfer modeling. The vertical aerosol profiles (AEROPs) from the aerosol and carbon detection lidar (ACDL) aboard the Daqi-1 (DQ-1) satellite, atmospheric profiles (ATMSPs) from ERA5 reanalysis, and surface albedo from Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) products to drive Santa Barbara DISORT Atmospheric Radiative Transfer (SBDART) simulations. The resulting radiance fields were then used to retrieve the SVR across diverse regions and viewing geometries. An aerosol classification algorithm based on ACDL aerosol optical property products was developed and validated against Cloud-Aerosol Lidar and Infrared Pathfinder Satellite Observation (CALIPSO) vertical feature mask (VFM) product. The intercomparison shows higher consistency for dust-related subtypes where depolarization provides stronger constraints, whereas confusion persists between pollution (PL) and smoke (SM) due to overlapping optical signatures. ACDL aerosol optical profiles were validated through spatiotemporal collocation with aerosol robotic network (AERONET) measurements, yielding mean relative errors of 0.16% for single scattering albedo (SSA), 0.96% for asymmetry factor (ASY), and −8.37% for aerosol optical depth (AOD), suggesting that ACDL tends to underestimate column aerosol loading relative to AERONET. The SVR retrievals across six regions with diverse land covers and aerosol types showed strong spatial variability, driven by differences in aerosol vertical distributions, surface albedo, and observation geometry. The results demonstrate that the SVR decreases with increased aerosol extinction and its response to viewing angle is nonlinear and atmospheric condition dependent. In clean conditions, slant path extension can outweigh the rate of contrast attenuation, whereas in absorbing or mixed aerosol conditions, enhanced scattering and absorption elevate background radiance and accelerate apparent contrast decay, leading to decreasing SVR at larger zenith angles. The proposed SVR retrieval methodology enables multiangle SVR mapping, with potential applications in aviation safety, aerospace observation, and environmental assessment.
author: Wen Chu, Guangyao Dai, Wenrui Long, Chao Ren, Yufeng Wang, Fanqian Meng, Kangwen Sun, Shuangcheng Yang, Songhua Wu

title: Retrieving Lake Salinity From Microwave Imagery via Physical Mechanism Models
content: Salinity is one of the physicochemical property parameters in natural water, and its concentration directly affects the dielectric properties of lake water. Lake salinity microwave monitoring is insufficient now due to the limitations of spatial resolution for passive microwave radiometers and correcting lake surface roughness. Here, a physical mechanism lake salinity retrieval method is proposed, using synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery and thermal imagery as input satellite data, the modified Elfouhaily spectrum, the dielectric constant model, and a small perturbation method (SPM) as forward models, and stepwise derivation of salinity from Fresnel reflection coefficients after constructing a cost function to remove roughness contributions. The proposed model withstood validation with field and station salinity and showed high accuracy and robustness (N = 288, root mean square error (RMSE) = 0.77 ppt, and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) = 25.1%). Spatiotemporal patterns of salinity in 11 typical lakes in the Inner Mongolia-Xinjiang Lake (IMXL) were mapped from 2016 to 2024 using Sentinel-1 SAR data. The result showed that average salinity in spring (3.74 ppt) and autumn (3.69 ppt) exceeded these in summer (3.67 ppt), and the spatial patterns within the terminal lake commonly exhibit continuity and homogeneity. An examination of potential drivers emphasizes the dominant role of evaporation and precipitation on interannual salinity trends in most lakes. The low radiation forcing (SSP1–2.6) is the optimal sustainable pathway for IMXL in the 21st century, with salinity gradually declining after about three decades of development, meaning improved water quality.
author: Mingming Deng, Ronghua Ma, Lixin Wang, Minqi Hu, Kun Xue, Zhigang Cao, Junfeng Xiong

title: Toward the Improvement of SWIM Nadir Wind Speed Retrieval
content: Research on altimeter wind speed retrieval has shown that ocean wave parameters are important factors affecting the inversion of wind speed. Significant wave height (SWH) is typically used as a proxy for sea state to mitigate errors in altimeter wind speed retrieval, yet SWH alone fails to fully characterize ocean surface multiscale fluctuations, leaving residual wind speed inversion errors. The Surface Waves Investigation and Monitoring (SWIM) instrument, uniquely designed for ocean wave detection, provides wave parameters unavailable from traditional altimeters, offering an opportunity to advance wind speed retrieval techniques. Leveraging backpropagation neural networks (BPNNs), this study maximizes the contribution of wave parameters to enhancing the performance of nadir wind speed inversion to its fullest extent. Based on the conventional two-parameter wind speed algorithm, which utilizes the nadir normalized radar cross section (NRCS) and SWH to retrieve wind speed, two additional SWIM-derived parameters: the mean squared slope (MSS) of the ocean surface and the wave spectral level (WSL), are incorporated. Finally, eight different models are discussed in this study. The best one achieves a ~20% reduction in wind speed root mean square error (RMSE) compared to the version 7.0 SWIM Level2 nadir product. From the power spectral analysis, the introduction of MSS and WSL in fact enhanced the resolution capability of nadir wind speed, due to the reduction of the noise. In addition, a comprehensive analysis of the contributions of different wave parameters in wind speed retrieval is conducted. As a result, the MSS is the dominant factor in the retrieval, and the contribution of WSL is slightly greater than that of SWH. These findings underscore the potential of integrating more related wave parameters to refine satellite wind speed retrieval.
author: Weiping Hu, Bosen Jiang, Xiuzhong Li, Ying Xu, Shuyan Lang, Yijun He, Xinhua Zhao

title: EG-CMFNet: An Edge-Guided Cross-Modal Fusion Network for Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
content: Multimodal remote sensing data often exhibit complementary information about ground objects and are widely utilized for semantic segmentation tasks. However, spatial heterogeneity and misalignment from different modalities hinder the utilization of multimodal information. To address these challenges, an edge-guided cross-modal fusion network (EG-CMFNet) is proposed for multimodal remote sensing imagery. We introduce a multimodal adaptive feature interaction (MAFI) module to adaptively interact with modal information through dual-stage spatial–channel interactions. Furthermore, during the feature fusion stage, a lightweight feature fusion (LFF) module suppresses unimodal noise to achieve efficient fusion of multimodal complementary information. Additionally, an edge auxiliary (EA) module is designed to extract boundary features precisely, mitigating confusion between semantic and edge information. Comparative experiments demonstrate that EG-CMFNet achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness across three datasets of varying scales, including WHU-OPT-SAR, Dongying, and Pohang datasets. Specifically, EG-CMFNet achieves the highest mean intersection over union of 56.05%, 94.68%, and 97.51%, as well as overall accuracy (OA) of 84.47%, 94.97%, and 97.72%, respectively. The source code is available at https://github.com/masurq/EG-CMFNet
author: Jinqi Zhao, Zhonghuai Zhou, Liansong Zhang, Linxin Wang, Zhong Lu

title: High-Quality Small Fire Samples Generation and Application in Deep Learning-Based Forest Fire Monitoring
content: Early fire detection is critical for disaster mitigation and ecological safety. Geostationary satellites, with their high-temporal-resolution data, offer key support for near-real-time fire monitoring. However, the lack of high-quality fire-sample datasets, especially for small fires, and the underuse of fire-related spectral-spatiotemporal features limit the performance of current algorithms, leading to higher omission errors and reduced effectiveness for early warning. To overcome these limitations, this study introduces a multi-source satellite cross-verification strategy to generate a representative small-fire sample dataset. The thermal characteristics of fires are fully exploited by extracting and integrating the temporal evolution of five key features derived from 3.9 to  $11.2~\mu $ m brightness temperatures, including their absolute values, interchannel difference, and spatial-contextual differences. Furthermore, we develop LEAP-transformer, a transformer-based detection framework tailored for small-fire signals, which achieves the best accuracy and recall against four representative deep-learning models. Evaluated on a dedicated test dataset and real fire cases, LEAP-transformer significantly outperforms the Himawari-9 Level-2 wildfire product (L2WLF), achieving an accuracy of 95.3%. In 25 confirmed fire events, the LEAP-transformer detected 44% of the fires earlier than L2WLF, with an average lead time of 19 min. Ablation studies and SHAP-based interpretability analysis further validate the effectiveness of the selected fire features and the designed deep-learning framework. The proposed method provides an effective means to enhance the practical use of geostationary satellites for timely fire response.
author: Pixian Lv, Lin Sun, Xirong Liu

title: Structure-Guided Deep Learning for Depth-Domain Seismic Impedance Inversion
content: Deep learning (DL) has demonstrated significant potential for direct seismic impedance inversion in the depth domain. However, conventional DL frameworks often adopt a trace-by-trace strategy, which fails to account for the spatial coherence of subsurface structures, leading to lateral discontinuities and a lack of geological guidance. To address these limitations, we propose a dip-constrained multitrace deep neural network (DMDNN) for depth-domain seismic inversion. This approach integrates structural dip as a geological prior through a sliding window mechanism to quantify the spatial continuity of strata, thereby providing the explicit geological guidance missing in traditional methods. Unlike the standard multitrace input paradigm, the proposed method establishes physical correlations between adjacent traces via an implicit lateral constraint mechanism in the loss function, enabling effective multitrace collaborative inversion even with single-trace inputs. Furthermore, a Huber loss function is employed for label constraints to enhance the framework’s resilience to seismic noise, offering superior stability over standard mean squared error (mse) metrics. Extensive evaluations on synthetic and field datasets demonstrate that, with properly calibrated weights for the structural dip loss and constraint window sizes, the DMDNN significantly enhances lateral continuity and vertical resolution while maintaining high geological plausibility. By reducing dependence on large-scale training samples, this method provides a robust and geologically consistent pathway for reservoir characterization in complex structural zones.
author: Jian Zhang, Yiran Xue, Hui Sun, Xingguo Huang, Xinyu Xia, Shuaiyang Wang

title: KAN-UG: Kolmogorov–Arnold Network-Based Difference Feature Enhancement Method With Uncertainty Guidance for Change Detection
content: Although existing semi-supervised methods for remote sensing change detection (RSCD) have shown promising performance with limited labeled data, they still face two key challenges. First, due to the complexity of remote sensing (RS) scenarios, these methods often struggle to capture subtle changes of ground objects and exhibit limited capacity to model the nonlinear characteristics of complex objects, thereby restricting detection accuracy. Second, existing methods fail to adequately exploit the collaboration between labeled data and unlabeled data, which tends to cause error accumulation and consequently leads to insufficient representation of challenging regions. Recently, Kolmogorov–Arnold network (KAN) has attracted increasing attention for its strong capability in modeling complex nonlinear data distributions. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Kolmogorov–Arnold network (KAN)-based difference feature enhancement method with uncertainty guidance (KAN-UG) for semi-supervised RSCD. First, a KAN-based difference feature enhancement module (K-DFEM) is constructed to better capture subtle changes in complex scenarios. This module enhances the discriminability of features in complex scenarios through learnable nonlinear activation and progressive multiscale fusion. Second, an uncertainty-guided adaptive learning (UAL) mechanism is designed to prevent the model from being misled during optimization. This mechanism promotes effective collaboration between supervised and unsupervised learning by improving the utilization of challenging regions in labeled data and mitigating error propagation from challenging regions in unlabeled data. Experimental results on four widely used datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art semi-supervised change detection (SSCD) methods across diverse and challenging scenarios. In particular, with only 5% labeled ratio, our method achieves intersection over union (IoU) improvements of 7.42% and 3.70% over the best competing method on the CDD and SYSU-CD datasets, respectively. The code will be available at https://github.com/CAG-kong/KAN-UG
author: Shaoxiong Lin, Tao Lei, Hailong Ning, Xingwu Wang, Xuan Wang, Daqi Liu, Maoguo Gong, Asoke K. Nandi

title: Toward Stable Semi-Supervised Remote Sensing Segmentation via Co-Guidance and Co-Fusion
content: Semi-supervised remote sensing (RS) image semantic segmentation offers a promising solution to alleviate the burden of exhaustive annotation, yet it fundamentally struggles with pseudo-label drift, a phenomenon where confirmation bias leads to the accumulation of errors during training. In this work, we propose Co2S, a stable semi-supervised RS segmentation framework that synergistically fuses priors from vision-language models (VLMs) and self-supervised models (SSMs). Specifically, we construct a heterogeneous dual-student architecture comprising two distinct ViT-based vision foundation models initialized with the weights of pretrained CLIP and DINOv3 to mitigate error accumulation and pseudo-label drift. To effectively incorporate these distinct priors, a semantic co-guidance mechanism is introduced that utilizes text embeddings and learnable queries to provide explicit and implicit class-level guidance, respectively, thereby jointly enhancing semantic consistency. Furthermore, a global–local feature collaborative fusion strategy is developed to effectively fuse the global contextual information captured by CLIP with the local details produced by DINOv3, enabling the model to generate highly precise segmentation results. Extensive experiments on six popular datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, which consistently achieves leading performance across various partition protocols and diverse scenarios. Project page is available at https://xavierjiezou.github.io/Co2S/
author: Yi Zhou, Xuechao Zou, Shun Zhang, Kai Li, Shiying Wang, Jingming Chen, Congyan Lang, Tengfei Cao, Pin Tao, Yuanchun Shi

title: Modeling Dataset-Level Priors With Learnable Probability Tables for Pansharpening
content: Pansharpening is a crucial technique for enhancing the resolution of multispectral images by fusing high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) images with low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) images. While recent methods, such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformers, have made progress by leveraging sample-level local and global information, they overlook the statistical structure shared across the entire dataset, which we refer to as the dataset-level priors. In this work, we propose a novel framework, the learnable probability table-guided pansharpening network (LPTNet), which models dataset-level priors through learnable probability tables to guide the pansharpening process. Two core components, the probability value table (PVT) and the probability look-up table (PLT), are introduced to learn a compact representation of the global distribution of multispectral features. The PVT discretizes the space of high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) features into a compact set of basis vectors, and the PLT assigns corresponding probabilities to these vectors. Specifically, the probability associated with each vector in the PVT is derived by measuring the similarity between the pixel-wise features of the LRMS and the vectors in the PLT. The HRMS features for each pixel are then reconstructed as the expectation over this discrete distribution. To refine these priors across different scales, the PVT and PLT are evolved through scale-aware updates conditioned on multiscale features, enabling the model to integrate progressively dataset-level priors with local context information. Extensive experiments on QuickBird (QB), WorldView-2 (WV2), and GaoFen-2 (GF2) datasets demonstrate that LPTNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, notably reaching a Q2n score of 0.8974 and a spectral angle mapper (SAM) of 0.7837 on the QB dataset, which significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of both spectral and spatial fidelity. The code is available at https://github.com/ShaowuCh/LPTNet
author: Shaowu Wu, Lihui Chen, Lihua Jian, Gemine Vivone, Kejiang Xiao, Xiaoguang Niu

title: Dynamic Context-Topology-Guided Cross-Modal Traversing Mamba for Pan-Sharpening
content: Pan-sharpening aims to fuse the texture-rich details of a high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) image with the spectral information of a low-resolution multispectral (MS) image to generate a high-resolution MS (HRMS) counterpart. Recently, state-space models (SSMs), notably the Mamba architecture, have emerged as promising alternatives for global sequence modeling, achieving efficient processing through inherent linear computational complexity. However, existing Mamba-based pan-sharpening approaches suffer from two critical limitations. First, context-agnostic scanning flattens 2-D images into 1-D sequences using a fixed, predetermined order. This rigid process fails to adapt to the varying distribution of spatial contexts, thereby disrupting intrinsic spatial relationships and severely compromising local contextual coherence. Second, unimodal sequential modeling processes PAN and MS features through separate pathways, limiting cross-modal interaction to simple concatenation at the final network stages. This implicit fusion mechanism fails to establish fine-grained dependencies between the PAN and MS modalities. To address these challenges, we propose the context-topology-guided cross-modal traversing Mamba (CTGMamba), a novel pan-sharpening framework that seamlessly integrates spatial context awareness and cross-modal semantic interaction into the SSM paradigm. First, a spatial context-aware module dynamically derives the 2-D contextual topology of the intrinsic image structures to determine a content-adaptive scanning order, preserving spatial coherence during sequence flattening. Second, leveraging the inherent spatial isomorphism between modalities, a cross-modal interleaving mechanism performs structural reorganization of the cross-modal sequence by aligning and interleaving PAN and MS tokens based on their semantic correspondence, enabling fine-grained cross-modal interaction synchronized with the scanning trajectory. Extensive experiments over multiple satellite benchmarks demonstrate that our proposed CTGMamba achieves state-of-the-art performance in both spatial and spectral fidelity.
author: Wenyang Zhou, Jiangtao Cui, Xiaofang Xia, Man Zhou, Min Zhao, Xuanhua He, Danfeng Hong

title: Region-Aware MoE Network for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
content: Existing transformer-based hyperspectral image (HSI) and multispectral image (MSI) fusion methods often suffer from limited cross-modal interaction and insufficient capability to model heterogeneous spatial and spectral patterns across different regions, which restricts their ability to fully exploit modality complementarity. To address these issues, we propose a region-aware mixture of experts (MoE) network for HSI and MSI fusion. The proposed framework consists of three key components. First, an attention sharing module (ASM) is designed to explicitly propagate attention information between modalities, enabling more balanced interaction between spatial and spectral representations. Second, the region-aware MoE (RAMoE) is introduced, where multiple region-aware experts (RAEs) capture region-specific feature variations, while an invariant-weight expert (IWE) provides global contextual guidance, improving robustness and generalization. Third, a spatial and spectral reconstruction module (SSRM) is employed to jointly recover spatial details and spectral information through residual learning. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art fusion methods in terms of both quantitative metrics and visual quality. Furthermore, experiments on real-world datasets verify the practical effectiveness of RAMoE in recovering fine spatial details while preserving spectral fidelity. The code will be released at https://github.com/XXn25/RAMoE
author: Nan Xiao, Xiyou Fu, Qi Ren, Wangquan He, Siqi Wei, Sen Jia

title: Multiscale Spiking Graph Convolution Aggregation Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification remains challenging due to complex spectral–spatial patterns, the growing demand for energy-efficient modeling in resource-constrained remote sensing applications, and the structural limitations of existing graph convolutional network (GCN)- and spiking neural network (SNN)-based methods. Conventional GCNs rely on static graphs and coarse neighborhood aggregation, limiting their ability to model non-Euclidean spectral–spatial relations, while most SNN-based approaches exploit event-driven sparsity for low-energy computation but lack effective adaptive multiscale and structured relational modeling. To address these issues, we propose a multiscale spiking graph convolution aggregation network (MS2GCAN) that integrates spike-driven temporal dynamics with adaptive graph learning. MS2GCAN comprises a multiscale temporal enhancement module for fine-to-coarse spatial encoding, an adaptive spiking graph convolution (ASGC) module with feature-dependent dynamic graphs, and a center spatiotemporal aggregation (CSTA) module for multihop spatial fusion with learnable temporal weighting. Experiments on three public hyperspectral datasets demonstrate that MS2GCAN achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance with only 0.06–0.18 M parameters. In addition, the proposed model exhibits sparse spiking activity across layers, indicating strong potential for energy-efficient computation. These results validate the effectiveness of MS2GCAN for accurate and resource-efficient HSI classification (HSIC). The source code will be released at https://github.com/fwx0314/MS2GCAN
author: Shufang Xu, Wenxuan Fei, Gaoxu Wang, Runhua Sheng, Zhonghao Chen, Hongmin Gao

title: Fast Anchor Graph Regularized Relaxation Linear Regression for Classification of Hyperspectral Images
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification remains challenging due to the inherently high dimensionality and complex intrinsic structure of the data. These characteristics pose significant difficulties for traditional linear regression (LR) methods, particularly when it comes to capturing nonlinear relationships and preserving local structural information. To address these issues, this article proposes a novel classification framework called fast anchor graph regularized relaxation LR (FAGRLR). The proposed method integrates a fast anchor graph (AG) model into LR to encode both spatial and structural neighborhood information inherent in HSIs. By utilizing AGs, the method efficiently preserves critical local relationships within the data, enhancing the discriminative ability of the model. To further avoid overfitting, a label relaxation mechanism is introduced to increase the label matrix’s flexibility in discriminative transformation learning. Additionally, the  $L_{2,1}$ -norm constraint is incorporated to promote robustness and sparsity in the learned transformation matrix, thereby enhancing the generalization performance of the proposed method. We validate the effectiveness of FAGRLR through extensive experiments conducted on four benchmark hyperspectral datasets. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior classification performance compared to several state-of-the-art methods.
author: Chenfeng Long, Mengxin Zhang, Yang-Jun Deng, Longfei Ren, Heng-Chao Li, Qian Du

title: Dynamic Contrastive Learning for Hierarchical Retrieval: A Case Study of Distance-Aware Cross-View Geo-Localization
content: Existing deep learning-based cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) methods primarily focus on improving the accuracy of cross-domain image matching. Less attention is paid to ensuring that models can comprehensively capture contextual information around the target and minimize the cost of localization errors. To support quantitative research into this distance-aware CVGL (DACVGL) problem, we construct a distance-aware campus (DA-Campus), the first benchmark that pairs multiview imagery with precise distance annotations across three spatial resolutions. Based on DA-Campus, we formulate DACVGL as a hierarchical retrieval problem across different domains. In this setting, we further identify that due to the inherent complexity of spatial relationships among buildings, conventional metric learning lacks a unified semantic hierarchy to guide the organization of the latent feature space. To tackle this challenge, we propose dynamic contrastive learning (DyCL), a novel framework that progressively aligns feature representations according to spatial margins. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DyCL can serve as a strong baseline for the DACVGL task, yielding substantial improvements in both hierarchical retrieval performance and overall geo-localization accuracy. Our code and benchmark are publicly available at https://github.com/anocodetest1/DyCL
author: Suofei Zhang, Xinxin Wang, Xiaofu Wu, Quan Zhou, Haifeng Hu

title: Rapid Assessment of Earthquake-Induced Building Damage Using Optical Image Correlation
content: Rapid assessment of earthquake-damaged buildings—such as area, quantity, and severity—is crucial for timely post-earthquake rescue. Pre- and post-earthquake data collected from optical remote sensing systems can effectively resolve earthquake deformation. Among them, the normalized correlation coefficient reduction (NCCR) shows promise in directly reflecting building damage. Here, we propose an automated processing chain for building damage detection, which integrates subpixel image correlation technique, deep learning-based segmentation and anomaly detection models, and a weight stacking method. We applied this chain to assess building damage in 77 cities affected by the 2023 Türkiye earthquake doublet. Our results demonstrate that the method effectively and accurately detects earthquake-induced building damage, aligning closely with officially reported damage and peak ground acceleration (PGA) data. By comparing our findings with official damage degree data from Kahramanmaraş, we constrained the NCCR thresholds for five damage levels: no damage (0.21), slight damage (0.37), moderate damage (0.50), serious damage (0.71), and destroyed (0.82). In addition, we found that cities on the fault hanging wall and footwall need to be over 23 and 12 km away from the fault, respectively, to experience low damage levels (NCCR below 0.16). This reveals significant differences in building damage between the two fault walls, improving our understanding of the relationship between building damage and proximity to causative faults. Our proposed methodology holds great potential for rapidly providing critical building damage information, thereby supporting efficient post-earthquake rescue and emergency response efforts.
author: Chenglong Li, Yanxing Wu, Xinjian Shan, Zhangfeng Ma, Xiaogang Song, Guohong Zhang, Shengji Wei

title: A Lightweight Wavelet-Aligned Difference and Mask-Guided Fusion Network for Change Detection
content: Remote sensing (RS) change detection (CD) plays an important role in many application domains. To meet the practical demand for both high accuracy and efficiency, and address the fundamental challenges of bitemporal misalignment, insufficient difference representation, and change and nonchange coupling, this article proposes a lightweight wavelet-aligned difference and mask-guided feature fusion network (WDMF-Net). WDMF-Net first employs a Siamese MobileNetV2 with an improved adjacent-level aggregation module (AAM) to extract multiscale complementary features. A wavelet-enhanced feature alignment module (WFAM) is then introduced to enhances local boundaries and global structures of these features in frequency domain, followed by context-consistent spatial alignment to reduce bitemporal misregistration. On this basis, a collaborative feature difference module (CFDM) is designed to model the intermediate complementary relationship between channel and spatial dimensions, enabling accurate localization and capture of contextual differences. Finally, a mask-guided feature enhancement module (MFEM) is employed, where foreground and background mask priors are progressively involved in multiscale difference fusion to explicitly decouple changed and unchanged regions. Experiments demonstrate that WDMF-Net outperforms 16 SOTA methods on five public CD datasets and achieve a favorable balance among detection accuracy, computational efficiency, and generalization capability. The code is available at https://github.com/LYT-Works/WDMF-Net.
author: Yuting Liu, Shihua Li, Lorenzo Bruzzone

title: Correntropy-Based Robust Blind Seismic Deconvolution
content: Blind deconvolution is a well-established technique in seismic data processing, used to estimate subsurface reflectivity from recorded signals. However, traditional methods often rely on the  $\ell _{2}$ -norm and assume Gaussian noise, which can limit their performance in real seismic environments, where impulsive and non-Gaussian noise may occur. In order to address this limitation, this work proposes a robust blind deconvolution approach based on a correntropy cost function. By replacing the traditional  $\ell _{2}$ -norm with a correntropy criterion, the method accounts for higher order statistical information and becomes robust to outliers and non-Gaussian noise. The algorithm alternates between estimating the reflectivity signal and the source wavelet, based on the alternating minimization sparse multichannel blind deconvolution (AM-SMBD) framework. The method is evaluated on synthetic data under noiseless, Gaussian, and impulsive noise conditions, as well as on real seismic records. Results show that the proposed approach performs similarly to the  $\ell _{2}$ -based method under Gaussian noise and outperforms it in environments with  $\alpha $ -stable non-Gaussian noise. It was also able to better preserve reflectors and attenuate noise in real seismic data.
author: Tales Câmara, Kenji Nose-Filho, Pedro O. A. Santos, Sérgio Luiz E. F. da Silva, Bruno César Zanardo Honório, Jorge Luiz Matias, Samuel Xavier-de-Souza, Tiago Barros, Luiz F. Silveira

title: An Energy-Adaptive Chirp-Enhanced W-Transform Incorporating Chirp-Compensating Phase Modulation for High-Resolution Seismic Time-Frequency Analysis
content: Seismic signals display nonstationary properties essential for characterizing subsurface formations, including fluid saturation, lithological variations, and structural details. Conventional time-frequency analysis (TFA) techniques often yield suboptimal resolution due to energy dispersion and inadequate adaptation to signal complexity. The W-transform (WT) addresses some limitations by dynamically adjusting the Gaussian window to dominant frequencies, but challenges persist in achieving optimal performance across diverse signal conditions. A chirp-enhanced WT (CEWT) advances this approach by integrating energy-based window refinement and adaptive phase modulation to better capture local nonstationarity. This framework produces more concentrated time-frequency representations (TFRs), enhances robustness to noise, and enables accurate signal reconstruction. Synthetic tests involving Morlet wavelets, Ricker wavelet pairs, and noise-contaminated traces validate CEWT’s superior performance in resolution and efficiency compared to standard and normalized WTs (NWTs). Real-world applications on a marine dataset from the South Caspian Sea highlight improved low-frequency shadow (LFS) detection for hydrocarbon indicators, while an onshore profile from the Dagang oil field demonstrates enhanced reservoir characterization through sharper iso-frequency sections and RGB composites, outperforming existing methods in anomaly delineation and stratigraphic imaging.
author: Abtin Pegah, Bo Feng, Amin Roshandel Kahoo, Gang Yu, Ehsan Pegah, Huazhong Wang

title: Full-Waveform-Accurate 3-D Inversion for Transient Electromagnetics via Parallel Spectral Methods
content: Conventional three-dimensional (3-D) inversion of transient electromagnetic (TEM) data predominantly employs time-stepping forward modeling, which suffers from inherent limitations in parallelization efficiency and high computational costs. These drawbacks become particularly pronounced when incorporating full transmitting-current waveforms, especially those involving waveform repetitions. To address these challenges, this study proposes a 3-D inversion method based on spectral forward modeling. By employing message passing interface (MPI)+OpenMP hybrid parallel programming technology, efficient parallel computation of magnetic field responses and Jacobian matrices along the frequency dimension is achieved. In addition, a convolution algorithm is incorporated to integrate the effects of waveform repetition into the forward modeling of full-waveform transmitting current. Validation with synthetic data examples demonstrates that, compared to traditional time-stepping methods, the spectral method exhibits significant advantages in both forward modeling and inversion efficiency. The study also reveals that full-waveform inversion can more accurately reconstruct subsurface electrical structures, with magnetic field (B-field) data inversion showing markedly higher sensitivity to identifying good conductors in low-resistivity environments compared to induced electromotive force (EMF) data. Furthermore, the inclusion of full-waveform information not only improves inversion accuracy but also significantly enhances overall computational efficiency by accelerating residual convergence and reducing iteration counts. In practical applications, inversion results from exploration data in the Qingchengzi ore concentration area, Liaoning Province, indicate that while the overall geoelectric structures derived from EMF and B-field data inversion are consistent, the B-field data provide more accurate delineation of the vertical extent of deep good conductors. These findings align closely with previous research, fully demonstrating the unique advantages of magnetic field data in deep exploration.
author: Yao Wang, Jianhui Li, Yinjia Zhu, Hongzhu Cai, Junjie Wu, Xingchun Wang

title: A Label-Adaptive Smoothing Method With Multiscale Iterative Strategy for Ground-Satellite Image Geo-Localization
content: Cross-view image geo-localization aims to estimate a camera's 3-DoF pose (location and orientation) by comparing the captured ground-level image to a geo-referenced overhead satellite image that covers the camera's local surroundings. However, this task remains challenging due to the significant appearance and viewpoint discrepancies between ground and aerial images in dense urban environments, which often lead to substantial pose uncertainty. To overcome this challenge, we propose a novel label-adaptive smoothing method with a multiscale iterative strategy that integrates uncertainty-aware label distribution modeling into the training process at multiple spatial resolutions, enabling more robust alignment of cross-view features and improving the accuracy of the pose estimation. Specifically, we design a fine-grained feature alignment module to predict both the mean and variance of the continuous pose distribution, which are then used to compute an unimodal-concentrated loss. However, due to varying levels of prediction uncertainty across different samples, a fixed weighting between the unimodal loss and the concentrated loss is insufficient to capture such dynamics, which may lead to unstable or low-confidence predictions. Therefore, we introduce a negative feedback-based label distribution refinement module, which adjusts the relative strength of the unimodal-concentrated loss based on the validation-set mean absolute pose error to adaptively balance the contributions of the unimodal loss and the concentrated loss, thereby enabling confident and precise pose predictions in complex urban environments. Extensive experiments on VIGOR and KITTI demonstrate that our method reduces median localization error by 41% over strong baselines, and achieves 53.05% and 63% reductions in distributional localization and orientation errors.
author: Zhao Zeng, Zhen Zhou, Qing Zhu, Yaonan Wang, Tianming Li

title: DBFITNet: A Dual-Branch Feature Interaction Transformer Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Existing dual-branch networks integrating convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformers have achieved remarkable performance in hyperspectral image classification. However, most methods simply combine the spectral–spatial features, neglecting the deep fusion of spectral–spatial correlations and lacking effective bidirectional interaction between the two branches. To address these issues, we propose a novel dual-branch feature interaction transformer network (DBFITNet), which adopts a dual-branch architecture based on a spatial–spectral fusion transformer encoder (SSFTE) and a local masked selective transformer encoder (LMSTE). Specifically, the SSFTE achieves adaptive deep fusion of spatial–spectral features through a weight attention strategy. Meanwhile, the LMSTE enhances local details while reducing interference from redundant features through a masked selection strategy. Besides, the DBFITNet adopts two complementary interaction modules of transformer–CNN integration module and CNN–transformer integration module to enable bidirectional feature interaction and collaborative optimization across branches. Finally, a multipath fusion module aggregates the enriched features to generate highly discriminative joint representations. Experiments on four public datasets demonstrate that the proposed DBFITNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior classification accuracy with robust performance under limited training samples.
author: Haixin Sun, Jian Yu, Fanlei Meng, Jiaqi Liang, Yiran Chen

title: Precise LiDAR-Only Odometry With Error Rectification in Structured Environments
content: Accurate self-localization is a prerequisite for advanced autonomous systems. LiDAR odometry provides a relative solution to this problem yet inevitably drifts over time. So far, many efforts have been made to reduce the drift, such as introducing semantic information. However, existing methods neglect fully exploiting the measurement uncertainties when formulating the LiDAR odometry, which also improves the accuracy as demonstrated in this article. The proposed LiDAR odometry method uses planes as landmarks for point cloud registration. With plane-to-plane correspondences, we estimate the relative pose as well as the error terms of plane parameters. To this end, we establish the correlations between planes via an empirical covariance function. The error rectification for plane parameters enables us to maintain a high quality plane map and thus suppress the drift. We further refine the pose using point-to-plane correspondences and acquire a low-drift LiDAR odometry (and mapping) system. The validation of our method is conducted on different datasets that involve challenging urban and indoor scenes. Experimental results suggest that our method is competitive among state-of-the-art methods, achieving accurate pose estimation on the fly.
author: Bofeng Li, Linkun He, Guang’e Chen

title: Retrieving Tropical Cyclone Radius of Maximum Wind From Geostationary Satellite Infrared Imagery With SAR Observations as Ground Truth
content: The radius of maximum wind (RMW) is a critical parameter for characterizing the tropical cyclone (TC) inner core structure, wind field distribution, and potential hazard extent. While spaceborne cross-polarized synthetic aperture radar (SAR) enables high-precision wind field measurements, its high operational cost and low temporal resolution restrict widespread application. To address this challenge, this study develops an RMW retrieval algorithm leveraging geostationary satellite infrared imagery, with SAR-derived wind products serving as ground truth. Focusing on TCs over Western North Pacific during 2016–2024, a dataset comprising 110 eyed and 121 noneye TC samples was curated from quality-controlled SAR observations. The proposed algorithm extends the eye-radius-based RMW estimation method, which was originally designed for clear-eyed TC, to unclear-eyed scenarios. Simultaneously, it introduces a novel proxy based on the radius of strongest convection for retrieving RMW in noneye TCs. Rigorous evaluation through independent validation, cross-validation, and comparative analysis confirms that the algorithm achieves superior accuracy relative to the Joint Typhoon Warning Center best-track records. Ablation experiments and sensitivity tests examining satellite data sources, temporal asynchrony, eyewall replacement cycles, and center positioning accuracy, further substantiate the robustness and stability of the proposed algorithm. This work establishes an operationally feasible and scalable framework for high-accuracy, high-frequency monitoring of TC inner core dynamics, with direct implications for advancing forecasting and hazard assessment capabilities.
author: Tianhao Chen, Xiaoyong Zhuge, Hui Yu, Duanyang Liu

title: Intelligent Tree Canopy Segmentation From UAV Imagery to Support Urban and Rural Garden Design
content: Accurately segmenting the tree canopies from autonomous aerial vehicle (AAV) images is a crucial prerequisite for conducting canopy morphology analysis and performing precise pruning in intelligent gardens and orchards. However, canopy segmentation based on UAVs remains challenging due to the significant intraclass scale variations among tree canopies and the prominent outdoor lighting differences. These factors often result in blurred canopy boundaries, fragmented segmentation results, and limited robustness. Moreover, most existing methods were initially designed for ground scenes or trained under restricted imaging conditions, lacking effective optimization for multiscale feature representation and light interference adaptation in aerial agricultural scenarios. To address these challenges, this article proposes a tree canopy semantic segmentation network named Trans-CNN, which is based on a multiscale collaborative enhancement framework. First, a semantic-preserving color diversification strategy is introduced, which expands the distribution of training data through controlled color perturbations to enhance the model’s adaptability to lighting changes. Second, to mitigate the feature degradation during downsampling and improve the perception of scales, a cross-stage feature enhancement module is designed. It effectively fuses high-order and low-order features to enhance spatial positioning accuracy and the ability to depict tree canopies. Additionally, a multiscale attention map fusion module is introduced, which dynamically aggregates multiscale features through an adaptive attention mechanism to improve discrimination performance in complex vegetation backgrounds. A large number of experiments conducted on public aerial datasets and self-collected UAV tree canopy datasets demonstrate that the proposed Trans-CNN method outperforms existing methods. It provides a reliable and effective solution for UAV canopy segmentation and supports intelligent pruning and precise landscape management applications.
author: Liping Chen, Yao Chen

title: Hyperspectral Anomaly Detection via Tensor Multiple Subspace Learning With Gamma Tensor Correlated Total Variation
content: Recently, models that utilize tensor low-rank representation for hyperspectral anomaly detection (HAD) have demonstrated significant advantages. Nevertheless, such models still have deficiencies in accurately characterizing the low-rank and smooth coupling prior of the background and effectively expressing the group sparse structure of abnormal components. In addition, the extracted abnormal components are often easily disturbed by noise. To comprehensively address the above challenges, this article proposes a tensor multiple subspace learning (TMSL-GTCTV) model with gamma tensor correlated total variation (GTCTV) for HAD. First, a tensor multiple subspace learning scheme is adopted to model the background as a tensor product of a dictionary tensor and a coefficient tensor to characterize the multiple subspace distribution properties of the complex heterogeneous background. Then, by modeling the nonconvex gamma quasi-norm in the gradient domain related to the background, a GTCTV regularization is designed and applied to the coefficient tensor to enhance the expression of the low-rank and smooth coupling prior information of the background, thereby improving the accuracy of background modeling. To distinguish sparse nonanomalous targets, under the constraint of the tensor $\ell _{2,1,1}$-norm, a sparse optimization weight tensor for weighting abnormal tensor is designed. Meanwhile, the structured $\ell _{2,1}$-norm is adopted to model the noise component to enhance the ability to identify real abnormal targets. Furthermore, a robust dictionary tensor is constructed by combining GTCTV regularization and sparse optimization weight tensor and integrating them into the tensor robust principal component analysis framework. Finally, extensive experimental results on a synthetic and five real hyperspectral image datasets validate the superiority of our TMSL-GTCTV.
author: Qingjiang Xiao, Shuhan Chen, Risheng Huang, Liaoying Zhao, Xiaorun Li

title: A Two-Step Spatial Downscaling Method for Latent Heat Flux to Quantify Urban–Rural Gradients
content: Rapid urbanization has significantly altered urban land surface, impacting the surface energy balance and water cycles. As a key component of surface energy exchange, latent heat flux (LHF) plays a critical role in regulating urban climate. However, challenges persist in both accounting for urban surface heterogeneity in high-resolution LHF downscaling and quantifying urban–rural LHF gradients. This article proposes a two-step spatial downscaling method based on local climate zone as regression windows. First, coarse-resolution ERA5-Land LHF data (10 km) are integrated with 1 km intermediate products. Second, the data are further downscaled to 30m resolution. Based on the resulting high-resolution LHF dataset, urban–rural LHF gradients are quantified at annual, seasonal, and diurnal scales. The major findings are summarized as follows. 1) The downscaling results effectively capture spatial details related to water bodies, impervious surfaces, and topographic effects, showing strong consistency with high-resolution land use cover data. Validation against ground-station observations yields mean correlation coefficients of 0.91 at 1 km and 0.913 at 30m resolution. 2) The results reveal a clear urban–rural gradient, with annual LHF increasing 6.3 W/m2 from urban to rural. Pronounced seasonal and diurnal variations are observed, with the maximum urban–rural difference reaching 12.15 W/m2 in summer, and the urban–rural difference in LHF exhibits significant diurnal variation, the most pronounced contrast occurring during the daytime (reaching up to 14 W/m2), while the difference diminishes at night and nearly disappears. This article provides high-resolution LHF data to support urban climate modeling and sustainable urban planning.
author: Kangning Li, Xuelin Wang, Jinbao Jiang

title: $O^{2}Former$: Direction-Aware and Multiscale Query Enhancement for SAR Ship Instance Segmentation
content: Instance segmentation of ships in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is critical for applications such as maritime monitoring, environmental analysis, and national security. SAR ship images present challenges including scale variation, object density, and fuzzy target boundary, which are often overlooked in existing methods, leading to suboptimal performance. In this work, we propose O2Former, a tailored instance segmentation framework that extends Mask2Former by fully leveraging the structural characteristics of SAR imagery. We introduce two key components. The first is the Optimized Query Generator (OQG). It enables multiscale feature interaction by jointly encoding shallow positional cues and high-level semantic information. This improves query quality and convergence efficiency. The second component is the Orientation-Aware Embedding Module. It enhances directional sensitivity through direction-aware convolution and polar-coordinate encoding. This effectively addresses the challenge of uneven target orientations in SAR scenes. Together, these modules facilitate precise feature alignment from backbone to decoder and strengthen the model's capacity to capture fine-grained structural details. Extensive experiments demonstrate that O2Former outperforms state-of-the-art instance segmentation baselines, validating its effectiveness and generalization on SAR ship datasets.
author: Fei Gao, Yunrui Li, Xiaoyu He, Jinping Sun, Jun Wang, Amir Hussain

title: QueenNet: Quantum-Enhanced Neural Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification is a critical task in the field of remote sensing. Existing deep learning models have made significant progress in HSI classification, but often suffer from parameter redundancy and insufficient noise robustness when handling the high-dimensional nonlinear spatial-spectral information. Recently, quantum computing have garnered significant attention in modeling higher-order correlations with remarkable parameter efficiency. Inspired by this, we propose a QUantum-Enhanced nEural Network (QueenNet) for HSI classification. QueenNet comprises three primary modules, including Quantum Encoding (QE), Quantum Convolution (QC), and Quantum Measurement (QM). Specifically, QE maps HSI into an hyper high-dimensional Hilbert space, encoding it as a quantum states. QC performs HSI feature extraction and transformation using quantum circuits composed of parameterized quantum gate sequences, which effectively enhances the model's ability to capture joint spatial-spectral features while suppressing spectral noise. QM introduces nonlinear feature extraction operations and decodes the HSI features of quantum states back into classic interpretable probability distributions for classification. QueenNet effectively alleviate the limitations of existing HSI classification deep learning models by leveraging the high-dimensional feature representation advantages of quantum computing, introducing a novel paradigm to HSI classification. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that QueenNet achieves an order-of-magnitude reduction in model parameters while surpassing the classification performance of state-of-the-art methods.
author: Qingwang Wang, Shunyuan Wang, Yinxing Zhang, Jian Song, Kai Zeng, Tao Shen

title: Cross-modal Feature Fusion of Heterogeneous Remote Sensing Data for Improving Landslide Detection
content: Landslides triggered by earthquakes and extreme rainfall pose severe risks to human life and infrastructure, highlighting the need for rapid and accurate mapping after group-occurring landslides occur. The heterogeneous remote sensing data which has higher spectral dimensionality and greater complexity compared to conventional RGB imagery, provides diverse surface and structural information for advantageous discrimination of landslides from background areas. However, the heterogeneous remote sensing data for landslide detection is being underestimated. To solve the challenges for feature extraction and fusion from the heterogeneous remote sensing data, we presented a model named kernel-selective modal fusion network (KSMFNet), which is designed for landslide detection through cross-modal feature fusion. The proposed model includes a dual-branch architecture for extracting specific modality features and the fusion module to fuse modality features. The fusion module comprises two key components. First, a selective kernel submodule adaptively chooses suitable kernel sizes and modifies the receptive field for every spatial region on the input data. Second, a channel attention submodule is used to significantly improve the sensitivity of the model to specify channels, reinforce critical feature representations, and facilitate alignment of different modalities. The proposed model was initially pretrained on the large-scale dataset, followed by the transfer and fine-tuning of parameters in the two areas: 2018 Hokkaido coseismic landslides in Japan and 2024 Shaoguan rainfall-induced landslides in China. Results demonstrated that KSMFNet consistently outperformed both single-modal and two-branch models, which confirmed the robustness and generalizability of KSMFNet, underscoring the value of cross-modal fusion for reliable landslide detection across diverse geological environments.
author: Wanqi Luo, Haijun Qiu, Dongdong Yang, Wenchao Huangfu, Guoqing Yang, Kailiang Zhao

title: Physics-Aware Deep Fusion Framework and Benchmark for SAR Target Classification
content: The distinctive electromagnetic scattering characteristics of SAR target images are crucial for SAR target recognition. However, existing purely data-driven paradigms often overlook inherent physical scattering laws, which limits model robustness and interpretability. To address this issue, we propose a physics-aware interpretable framework, termed the attribute scattering center (ASC)-guided deformable capsule network. The proposed network adopts a two-stage learning framework.
