In the first stage, an ASC-guided capsule network is constructed to extract ASC capsules with explicit electromagnetic scattering semantics. Within the capsule architecture, we design a multiscale densely connected deformable module to adaptively capture target features at different scales. In addition, we design a polarity attribute feature perception module to enhance the exploitation of target attribute information. In the second stage, ASC capsules are embedded into deep attribute capsules to obtain a fused representation of physical scattering features and deep semantic features, thereby achieving reliable SAR target recognition. Furthermore, to further advance research on SAR target recognition, we construct and publicly release a new high-resolution benchmark dataset, termed ATR-SARVehicle-1.0 (https://doi.org/10.57760/sciencedb.j00001.01475), which covers multiple frequency bands and terrain backgrounds. Extensive experiments conducted on both the proposed benchmark and the MSTAR dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches in terms of recognition accuracy and interpretability.
author: Huiqiang Zhang, Wei Wang, Jie Deng, Zhongjie Wu, Sinong Quan, Jun Zhang

title: A Physically Informed Generalized Least Squares Fusion Framework for ECOSTRESS LST Validation Over Heterogeneous Landscapes
content: Validating new-generation land surface temperature (LST) products, such as the Ecosystem Spaceborne Thermal Radiometer Experiment on Space Station (ECOSTRESS), is constrained by sparse in-situ measurements that are insufficient to capture diverse atmospheric and surface heterogeneity. While Radiance-based methods offer extensive spatial coverage complementary to traditional Temperature-based approaches, establishing a unified validation benchmark remains challenging due to the lack of a rigorous framework to reconcile their inconsistent error structures. To address this limitation, a novel validation framework based on a Physically Informed Generalized Least Squares (PI-GLS) approach is proposed. Specifically, an error covariance matrix is analytically formulated based on radiative transfer theory to explicitly quantify the uncertainties inherent in each validation source and emissivity-induced correlations. By integrating this physical error structure into the GLS estimator, a statistically optimal validation metric is generated, enabling a unified and comprehensive accuracy assessment of ECOSTRESS LST products. Results reveal substantial performance variability across environmental gradients, with mean root mean square error (RMSE) ranging from 0.70 to 4.48 K. This variability underscores the sensitivity of validation metrics to sampling bias; spatially sparse sampling tends to bias error assessments, thereby necessitating the broad-coverage approach proposed herein. Applying the PI-GLS framework yields a validation RMSE of 2.33 K for ECOSTRESS. This metric provides a spatially representative assessment of sensor performance, effectively correcting the systematic underestimation observed in traditional equal-weighting (1.78 K) or weighted fusion (1.58 K) schemes that lack rigorous treatment of error correlations and sampling heterogeneity. Crucially, expanding validation coverage to previously under-sampled environments exposes systematic biases masked in limited local validations. Notably, diagnostic analysis points to inaccuracies in the atmospheric profiles used by ECOSTRESS as a likely source of the emissivity anomalies observed over the Tibetan Plateau. Beyond ECOSTRESS, this study establishes a scalable methodology to overcome in-situ data scarcity, providing a rigorous validation standard for future global TIR missions.
author: Yuting Qi, Lei Zhong, Yaoming Ma, Zixin Wang, Jie Liu

title: Adaptive Shortest Path Tracking for Robust Leaf–Wood Separation in Individual Trees From TLS Point Clouds
content: Leaf-wood separation is crucial for single-tree aboveground biomass estimation and three-dimensional reconstruction. Although the nondestructive and efficient acquisition of fine-grained, high-density point cloud data can be performed using terrestrial laser scanning technology, existing methods suffer from various drawbacks, including insufficient detection of fine branches, limited robustness to point cloud subsampling, and weak adaptability across different tree species and crown structures. A core issue lies in the over-reliance on prior values for key algorithm parameters. This article proposes an adaptive shortest path tracking for robust leaf–wood separation (ASPTS) in individual trees. First, a graph is constructed, and the shortest path backtracking is employed to extract skeleton points. Second, an improved k-nearest neighbor algorithm is proposed to adaptively optimize the number of neighboring points based on the shortest path, thereby obtaining initial wood points. Third, the feature descriptor construction for characterizing trunk and branch structures is optimized using principal component analysis by implementing an enhanced adaptive neighborhood radius selection strategy. Finally, final wood points are extracted using a region-growing approach guided by a stepwise feature thresholding scheme. A total of 22 individual trees, which represent different species, heights, and crown structures, are selected as test subjects. The results demonstrate the capability of ASPTS to make a good balance between type I and type II errors. ASPTS consistently exhibits strong fine-branch detection capability and robust performance under varying conditions, including different tree species, crown structures, and point cloud densities. ASPTS demonstrates superior performance compared to four state-of-the-art methods.
author: Yupeng Shen, Weifeng Ma, Chong Wang, Xiaodong Wu, Yuncheng Deng, Wanjing Yan

title: Multisemantics-Aware Optimal Feature Matching for Multimodal Remote Sensing Images
content: Feature matching for multimodal remote sensing images is a critical and challenging task due to the substantial differences in imaging mechanisms across diverse sensors. Traditional feature matching methods often struggle to achieve high accuracy under such conditions. To better exploit the structural and semantic information inherent in multimodal remote sensing data, we propose multisemantics-aware optimal feature matching (MSAMatch) method. MSAMatch incorporates multilevel and multisemantic attention mechanisms to enhance cross-modal feature extraction and information interaction, thereby significantly improving matching accuracy. Furthermore, we introduce a semantic-guided global matching strategy that integrates semantic cues with local features to enhance robustness. Finally, an optimal fine-level matching is achieved through bidirectional probabilistic modeling based on Gaussian distributions. Extensive experiments on several benchmark multimodal remote sensing datasets demonstrate that MSAMatch achieves state-of-the-art performance in feature matching tasks.
author: Yun Liao, Yide Di, Yunan Liu, Hao Zhou, Xuewen Tan, Junhui Liu, Qing Duan

title: SD-Net: A Synergistic Dual-Branch Network for Fine-Grained Building Extraction
content: Building footprint extraction from high-resolution remote sensing images (RSIs) is essential for applications, such as urban planning, disaster assessment, and infrastructure management. Existing methods often struggle to maintain semantic consistency while precisely delineating boundaries due to complex backgrounds and variations in building scale and morphology, leading to fractured contours, blurred edges, or mis-segmentation. To address these challenges, a synergistic dual-branch network (SD-Net) is proposed, which jointly models semantic and boundary information via a shared encoder with separate semantic and boundary decoders constrained by a joint loss, with gradient detachment to stabilize multitask optimization. A multiscale interaction module, the interactive dual-large kernel attention block, enhances context modeling for buildings of varying scales. Furthermore, a lightweight attention refinement cascade module refines high-level features by adaptively selecting discriminative channel and spatial information. Experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate that SD-Net outperforms mainstream segmentation models in mean Intersection over Union and F1-score and achieves more stable boundary integrity. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each module, confirming the method’s effectiveness and efficiency for resource-constrained automatic building extraction tasks.
author: Weidong Tao, Yanting Li, Zunfang Liu, Yidan Zhao

title: Spatiotemporal Thermal Deformation Error Calibration for Agile Optical Remote Sensing Satellite
content: With high maneuverability, the imaging range of agile optical remote sensing satellites can reach 45$^{\circ }$–60$^{\circ }$ from the nadir point, which significantly improves the temporal resolution and observation efficiency and is widely used in resource surveys, urban development, and disaster monitoring. However, due to the changes of the solar radiation energy and heated regions, there is the severe thermal elastic deformation in the payloads of agile satellites, which introduces the spatiotemporal thermal deformation errors. This article proposes a novel spatiotemporal error calibration method for agile optical remote sensing satellites based on thermal deformation factors. To express the variations in short-period and long-period thermal environments, and the heated regions, five categories of thermal deformation factors are constructed. Then, the spatiotemporal error calibration model with three hidden layers is established with two training optimization strategies. The complex and nonlinear relationship between thermal deformation factors and spatiotemporal error is established automatically without the prior analysis. The sample dataset for spatiotemporal error is established using the measurement data of star cameras and star sensors of LuoJia3-02 satellite. The dataset comprises 588 training samples and 146 test samples, with the data acquisition from 8 August to 26 November 2024 and the attitude angles from $-$35$^{\circ }$ to 40$^{\circ }$. The improvement rates of thermal deformation error along the optical axis angle, pitch angle, roll angle, and yaw angle are 53%, 69%, 56%, and 92%, respectively. The experiment results demonstrate the proposed method effectively corrects the spatiotemporal thermal deformation error.
author: Yanli Wang, Pinxiu Liu, Zihang Yang, Yingdong Pi, Mi Wang

title: Mapping Eucalyptus Plantations Age Using Phenology Knowledge-Aided Algorithms and GEE
content: Accurate and fast mapping Eucalyptus plantation age was vital for smart forestry management and economic development in Southern China. Traditional forest age detection approaches were focused on building regression relationships or allometric equations, limited by optical signal saturation. Current forest change detection approaches use long-term time series (LTSS) imagery to detect key forest disturbance and then generate age. However, Eucalyptus plantation suffered a short-rotation period and clear-cut phenology event dynamic, causing it a specific spectral trajectory. Hence, in this article, we proposed a novel Eucalyptus phenology knowledge-aided (EPKA) algorithm that considers specific phenology and detects the abrupt enhancement and rapid recovery of the integrated forest z-score index using Google earth engine. The spatial mapping of Eucalyptus plantation indicated that the EPKA algorithm achieved an overall accuracy of 93.33% by validation plots. The Eucalyptus age mapping indicated the spatial distribution of forest age, ranging from 0 to 13. Scatterplots between EPKA estimated and field surveyed disturbance year indicated that the EPKA achieved the accuracy of R2 = 0.96 and RMSE = 0.69 yr. The short-rotation pattern under different generations indicated that generation 1 of Eucalyptus plantation was the dominant proportion in the study area. This article demonstrated the benefits of the EPKA algorithm for mapping Eucalyptus plantation age in combination with phenology knowledge and LTSS change detection, which provided a promising algorithm for sustainable forest monitoring.
author: Hao Liu, Lifan Zhou, Pinghao Li, Kai Zhou, Kang Yang, Hanwen Yu, Wei Liu, Xiaolu Cheng

title: Integrating CCD and Adaptive-dNBR With Metaheuristic-Optimized Hybrid Deep Learning for Wildfire Detection and Susceptibility Mapping in Los Angeles County
content: Wildfires are among the most destructive natural disasters and pose significant risks to ecosystems, infrastructure, and human lives. This study focuses on wildfire susceptibility mapping in Los Angeles County, a region prone to severe wildfires, including the 2025 Palisades and Eaton wildfires. To enhance wildfire inventory mapping, we developed an integrated approach that combines coherence change detection (CCD) from Sentinel-1 SAR data and the adaptive-differenced normalized burn ratio (adaptive-dNBR) from Sentinel-2 optical imagery. This integration improved precision and recall to 0.97 and 0.90 compared with standalone methods (CCD = 0.75 and 0.97, dNBR = 0.78 and 0.98, adaptive-dNBR = 0.96 and 0.91). Using this improved inventory map as input data, we generated wildfire susceptibility maps employing a tree-based machine learning models—XGBoost and LightGBM, and deep learning models—a convolutional neural network (CNN), long short-term memory (LSTM), and hybrid CNN-LSTM—optimized with gray wolf optimization (GWO). The novel approach of metaheuristic-optimized hybrid CNN-LSTM model (CNN-LSTM-GWO) achieved the highest evaluation metrics (AUC = 0.958, Precision = 0.919, F1-score = 0.920, and MCC = 0.807), outperforming the standalone, nonoptimized models and tree-based machine learning. Key wildfire-related factors, such as maximum temperature, land use, solar radiation, humidity, wind speed, and slope, were identified as the most influential drivers of wildfire occurrence based on information gain ratio and Shapley additive eXplanations. Validation against newer wildfires (Bridge and Hudges) demonstrated strong alignment between the predicted high-risk zones and actual fire perimeters, confirming the model’s robustness for future wildfire prediction. This study represents a significant advancement in remote sensing-based wildfire analysis by integrating multisource satellite data with metaheuristic-optimized hybrid deep learning techniques. The proposed framework offers a powerful tool for proactive wildfire risk assessment and mitigation planning in Los Angeles County and other fire-prone regions.
author: Arief Rizqiyanto Achmad, Eunseok Park, Kwang-Jae Lee, Seung-Jae Lee, Sung-Ho Chae, Hahn Chul Jung, Yu-Chul Park, Chang-Wook Lee

title: Elevation-CVMamba: Elevation Estimation of Single-View Remote Sensing Image by Integrating CNN and VMamba
content: Accurate elevation estimation from a single remote sensing image is pivotal for applications such as 3-D city modeling. However, state-of-the-art (SOTA) deep learning methods face a critical trade-off: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) excel at preserving local details but struggle with global context, while Transformers capture long-range dependencies but often oversmooth fine structures and are computationally expensive. This architectural dilemma frequently leads to blurred elevation boundaries and structurally inconsistent estimations in complex scenes. To address this, we introduce elevation-CVMamba, an innovative dual-branch network that effectively synergizes these complementary strengths. One branch employs a CNN to preserve high-fidelity local textures, while the other leverages a vision Mamba encoder to efficiently model long-range spatial relationships. Critically, these parallel feature streams are intelligently merged by our novel content-aware channelwise fusion module, which learns to dynamically arbitrate the contribution of local and global features based on scene content. Extensive evaluations on the DFC2019, ISPRS Potsdam, and Vaihingen datasets demonstrate that elevation-CVMamba outperforms SOTA methods. Specifically, our approach reduces the MAE by 18.54% on DFC2019, 2.44% on Potsdam, and 14.75% on Vaihingen compared to the strongest baselines. This performance is achieved with exceptional efficiency, requiring 75.33% fewer parameters than a strong baseline such as DepthFormer. Qualitatively, the framework shows a superior ability to reconstruct distinct building boundaries and subtle terrain details, confirming its practical value for real-world applications.
author: Haiyan Pan, Hao Yu, Zhonghua Hong, Ruyan Zhou, Jiang Tao, Chenchen Jiang, Yanling Han, Yun Zhang

title: CondFusionSAR: A Bidirectional MultiConditional Fusion Network for Generating High-Resolution SAR Images With Complex Backgrounds
content: Currently, data-driven deep learning models advance slowly in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) ship image processing, largely because acquiring SAR images is quite challenging. Though SAR generation models offer a solution, their practical application value is often limited due to inadequate modeling of scene elements, poor controllability of ship positions, and ship offset or loss issues in complex scenes. To tackle the above challenges, we design a bidirectional multiconditional fusion network named CondFusionSAR with the location and class of targets as multiconditional inputs to controllably generate SAR ship images with complex backgrounds. We decouple SAR synthesis into bidirectional branches, namely, a ship synthesis branch and a background synthesis branch. The former adopts an encoder-decoder architecture with the proposed semantic injection module (SIM), which injects mask priors to address the issue of ships target loss in complex scene. The latter employs a mask-based diffusion model enhanced by mask-aware cross-attention (MaCA) to guide feature generation with the mask, and multiscale aggregation (MSA) to enhance background modeling capabilities. Moreover, a ship-background fuser with the spectral attention module (SAM) is designed to integrate outputs by applying learnable attention in both spatial and frequency domains, generating complex SAR image. Extensive experiments conducted on improved dataset show that CondFusionSAR can generate high-fidelity SAR ship images. Particularly, with the provided target annotation, the synthesized images can be directly used in other SAR ship tasks, which saves the time required for manual annotation.
author: Moran Ju, Mulin Li, Tengkai Mao, Si-Nian Jin

title: Best Practice for Integrating InSAR and GNSS Observations in High-Density GNSS Networks
content: Interferometric synthetic aperture radars (InSAR) is widely used to monitor ground deformation, but its results are relative to a local satellite-based reference system. To enable consistent geodetic interpretation, InSAR velocities must be aligned to a terrestrial reference frame, typically using Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) data. This study aims to identify the most effective referencing strategy to align ascending and descending InSAR datasets to the European terrestrial reference frame, using a dense GNSS network in a subsidence-affected area of northern Italy. We tested several referencing approaches by varying: first, the number and configuration of GNSS stations; second, the density of persistent scatterers (PS) used to calculate local InSAR velocities; third the modeling of InSAR-GNSS differences, using either a bias, a plane, or a second-order polynomial surface. Results show that the optimal referencing is achieved by modeling the differences with a planar surface, using all available GNSS stations, and computing InSAR velocities from PS within 100 m of each station. This approach minimized postreferencing residuals more effectively than other methods, including polynomial fitting. Following referencing, InSAR data from both geometries are merged to extract the vertical (Up) and horizontal (East) components of motion, demonstrating alignment with the GNSS-based reference system. These findings highlight how referencing performance depends on both the modeling strategy and GNSS-PS configuration, providing guidance for improving InSAR accuracy in geodetic applications.
author: Eugenia Giorgini, Milan Lazecky, Andrew Hooper, Stefano Gandolfi

title: Unsupervised Heterogeneous Remote Sensing Change Detection via Multiscale Mamba Attention and Feature Alignment
content: Heterogeneous change detection aims to identify land surface changes from multimodal remote sensing images acquired at different times. This task is particularly challenging due to inherent disparities in sensor imaging mechanisms, which result in inconsistent representations and complicate feature alignment, especially in the absence of labeled data. To address these challenges, we propose MambaHCD, a novel unsupervised framework that combines stacked convolutional denoising autoencoders with an encoder–decoder network equipped with multiscale Mamba attention modules. This architecture enables the extraction of noise-robust deep semantic features and efficient modeling of long-range spatial and modality-specific feature dependencies across different modalities. To guide the network training without labeled data, we introduce a composite loss function consisting of self-mapping loss, cross-mapping loss, and difference-map-based loss. This formulation jointly promotes intramodal and cross-modal feature alignment while adaptively guiding the network to distinguish between changed and unchanged regions, contributing to improved semantic discriminability under heterogeneous conditions. The final change map is produced by computing the L2-norm difference between reconstructed features, followed by refinement using dense conditional random fields and adaptive thresholding. Extensive experiments on public benchmark datasets show that the proposed method achieves competitive performance compared with existing state-of-the-art approaches, demonstrating its effectiveness for unsupervised heterogeneous change detection.
author: Gao Chen, Paixin Xie, Xueqian Wang, Qingfeng Zhou, Xiaoyan Li

title: Segmentation and Abundance Estimation of Deep-Sea Polymetallic Nodule Images Based on AttMSD-Uformer
content: With advances in seafloor sensor data acquisition and deep-learning-based image interpretation, modern deep-sea resource exploration has progressed substantially; however, traditional semantic segmentation models remain challenged on seafloor optical mineral imagery due to pronounced multiscale feature heterogeneity, object coalescence, and frequent missed detections of small-scale mineral targets. To this end, this study presents AttMSD-Uformer, a deep-learning semantic segmentation architecture that couples a dual-branch encoder with an attention-guided multiscale decoder and further introduces a semiautomated annotation pipeline that combines algorithmic recognition with expert-guided refinement to curate high-quality training datasets efficiently. The experimental results show that the proposed model delivers consistently strong segmentation performance across multiple seafloor mineral datasets and demonstrates robust cross-dataset generalization. Furthermore, this study systematically compared the predictive performance of four representative regression models using segmentation-derived image-level nodule coverage as the explanatory variable. The results indicate that the random-forest regressor is particularly well suited to the heterogeneous characteristics of seafloor polymetallic nodule imagery, excelling at modeling nonlinear and piecewise relationships. This methodology provides an efficient and reliable reference framework for intelligent quantitative assessment of seafloor resources.
author: Jiahao Zhang, Dongping Ming, Jianxin Xia, Leran Teng, Pengyi Yang, Chenglong Lai

title: CLIP-Enhanced Contrastive Clustering for Remote Sensing Image Retrieval
content: With the rapid growth of unlabeled remote sensing images, supervised learning methods relying on manual annotation have difficulty to meet the demands of large-scale data. Self-supervised contrastive learning provides an effective mechanism to leverage extensive unannotated datasets. However, these methods mainly focus on instance-level discrimination and ignore the underlying clustering distribution. To address this problem, we propose CLIP-enhanced contrastive clustering (CCC), a triple-branch framework designed for remote sensing image retrieval that utilizes the underlying clustering distribution to guide contrastive learning. Specially, CCC proposes a CLIP-based textual branch that constructs auxiliary cluster distributions using only category number, thereby forming a hybrid framework that couples visual contrastive clustering with text-derived semantic priors, eliminating the need for textual descriptions of remote sensing images. By designing a KL-divergence cluster center loss, we enhance intracluster compactness through text-guided visual cluster alignment. To better leverage the learning ability of the model, we also design a learning attention allocation strategy to shift focus from instance-level discrimination to cluster-level consistency. Experimental results show that CCC significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of mean average precision, average normalized modified retrieval rank, and P@k metrics, greatly reducing the reliance on costly manual annotations while maintaining robustness against inconsistent pseudolabeling.
author: Yan Zeng, Yun Ge, Jinlong Xu, Yuanyuan Shen

title: A Multidimensional Correlation Analysis Framework of SAR Target Recognition-Oriented Image Quality Enhancement
content: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) possesses significant advantages in automatic target recognition (ATR) due to its all-weather, day–night imaging capabilities. However, SAR-ATR faces complex challenges from inherent speckle noise and imaging-induced degradations, such as low contrast. These issues hinder feature extraction and compromise recognition accuracy. While existing SAR image quality enhancement methods improve visual quality, they often fail to align with the specific requirements of recognition. In this article, a multidimensional correlation analysis framework is proposed to systematically investigate the statistical relationships between SAR image quality metrics and recognition performance in order to bridge this gap. By integrating full-reference metrics, no-reference metrics, and task-driven accuracy evaluations, correlations are quantified using Pearson, Spearman, and Kendall coefficients to identify recognition-critical metrics. Then, a novel correlation constraint loss function is designed to jointly optimize noise suppression and discriminative feature preservation by directly embedding the recognition-critical metrics into the enhancement objective. A task-driven paradigm for recognition-oriented enhancement can be facilitated through the framework, where SAR image quality enhancement, image quality assessment, and downstream recognition are explicitly aligned. Experimental results on simulated and real-world low-quality datasets demonstrate that full-reference metrics, such as despeckling gain, exhibit strong positive correlations with recognition accuracy, while edge-focused no-reference metrics, such as Tenengrad, show negative correlations. Leveraging these insights, the proposed method improves average recognition accuracy by up to 9.06% on simulated data and achieves a peak gain of 4.03% under real-world conditions.
author: Chengyu Wan, Siqian Zhang, Zilong Chen, Yang Chen, Boli Xiong, Gangyao Kuang

title: Adaptive Threshold Detection of Cycle Slip Using the GWO-LSTM-GF Model in Low-Latitude Ionospheric Scintillation Environments
content: Phase measurements from the global navigation satellite system are fundamental to high-precision applications. However, in complex observation environments, these signals are susceptible to adverse propagation conditions, such as ionospheric scintillation and multipath effects. Adverse propagation conditions induce frequent cycle slips, causing discontinuities in carrier phase measurements. To this end, an adaptive cycle slip detection method is introduced, which is specifically tailored for ionospheric scintillation environments. Our approach integrates a grey wolf optimizer–long short-term memory model with geometry-free (GF) combinations to establish a dynamic detection threshold, which is informed by a predicted scintillation amplitude index. The method was validated using real-world BeiDou navigation satellite system (BDS) and global positioning system (GPS) data, where it demonstrated a significant reduction in false alarm rates compared with traditional GF combination methods. Specifically, false alarm rates decreased by over 30% (up to 83.3%) for BDS data and over 20% (up to 60.0%) for GPS data across various sampling rates.
author: Wuzheng Guo, Yuanfa Ji, Xiyan Sun, Xizi Jia

title: Single-Domain Generalization: A Scattering Characteristic-Driven Cross-Attention Model for Oriented SAR Object Detection
content: Oriented object detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery often struggles to generalize to unknown domains, due to severe distribution shifts. Single-domain generalization (single-DG) aims to tackle this challenge by learning robust representations from only one source domain. However, existing single-DG methods typically fail to capture the essential features of rotating objects, as they neglect the unique physical properties introduced by the SAR imaging process. To address this, this article proposes the scattering characteristic-driven cross-attention model (SCCAM), which introduces a heuristic scattering representation to approximate the potential physical structures of SAR objects. Specifically, a pair of homologous images is constructed by perturbing the extremely low- and high-frequency components of the input SAR image, ensuring that they share domain-invariant information. The heuristic attribute scattering center features are then extracted to guide a bidirectional cross-attention module, enabling progressive interactions between homologous features. This design effectively mitigates feature distribution discrepancies, thereby facilitating the learning of domain-invariant representations. Experimental results on six single-DG benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed SCCAM significantly outperforms multiple classic and state-of-the-art DG models in oriented object detection across unknown SAR domains.
author: Hailiang Huang, Huangxing Lin, Jingchao Guo, Yue Huang, Xinghao Ding, Huanqiang Zeng

title: Blind Hyperspectral Unmixing With Integrated Nonsmooth and CEM Spatial Constraints
content: Hyperspectral blind unmixing serves as a key technique in remote sensing image processing. Its core value lies in the ability to extract endmember spectral signatures and invert abundance fractions from mixed pixels without relying on prior endmember information. However, in practical applications, prevalent noise contamination in hyperspectral data often leads to degraded signal-to-noise ratio, which gives rise to spectral distortion and diminished image quality, consequently imposing a fundamental limitation on the accuracy of spectral unmixing. To overcome these challenges, this study introduces an innovative unmixing algorithm under the $\ell _{1}$ nonnegative matrix factorization framework that integrates endmember smoothness constraints with spatial abundance regularization based on constrained energy minimization (CEM). Specifically, a nonsmooth matrix is incorporated to enforce strong sparsity on endmembers, simultaneously smoothing image noise and improving endmember estimation accuracy. Furthermore, a novel dual-weight sparse regularization term is constructed by combining a CEM detector with a spatial weight, which concurrently enhances both the sparse representation and spatial continuity of abundance maps. On synthetic and real hyperspectral datasets, the proposed EISNMF algorithm yields greater accuracy in estimating both endmembers and abundances compared to existing blind unmixing techniques, proving to be particularly robust against high levels of noise.
author: Shaoquan Zhang, Kexing Li, Xinyi Zhou, Fan Li, Pengfei Lai, Lianhui Liang, Chengzhi Deng, Shengqian Wang

title: GF-1-Assisted Pseudolabel Augmentation and Leakage-Free Stacking for Sentinel-2 Chlorophyll-a Retrieval in Narrow Urban Rivers
content: Narrow urban rivers remain challenging for Sentinel-2–based chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) retrieval because mixed pixels, adjacency effects, and bank shadows can contaminate spectral signals, while in situ observations are often limited. This study develops an integrated workflow that integrates a high-confidence urban-river water mask with explicit shadow suppression to constrain feature extraction and sample selection, cross-resolution pseudolabel transfer by mapping a GF-1 Chl-a field to the Sentinel-2 10 m grid and stratifying it to form pseudolabeled samples for augmentation, and a leakage-controlled out-of-fold stacking regressor with an XGBoost metalearner to improve stability under data scarcity. On the in situ test set (n = 6), the GF-1 retrieval achieves R2 = 0.86 and RMSE = 3.32 μg/L. Using mapped pseudolabels for Sentinel-2 training, the model attains R2 = 0.84 and RMSE = 3.88 μg/L on a held-out pseudolabel set (n = 40), while the in situ only baseline yields R2 = 0.70 and RMSE = 4.47 μg/L on the separate in situ test set. These results indicate that cross-resolution pseudolabel augmentation and leakage-controlled ensembling can be effective under limited field supervision; the pseudolabel evaluation reflects internal consistency rather than independent ground truth.
author: Fanle Meng, Che Wang, Xuejun Wang, Luanbin Yin, Ning Lu, Jun Qin

title: Cross-Modal 3-D Gaussian Reconstruction: Fusing Image, Geometry, and Open-Vocabulary Semantics for Large-Scale Real-World Scenes
content: 3-D Gaussian Splatting (3-D GS) has revolutionized scene representation, efficient photorealistic rendering, and novel view synthesis. However, when applied to complex large-scale real-world scenes, 3-D GS faces two major challenges, which are geometric errors and rendering artifacts. To address these challenges, we propose FuseGS, a novel cross-modal Gaussian reconstruction framework that seamlessly fuses multiview images and dense LiDAR point clouds, along with open-vocabulary semantics from large vision–language models (VLMs) to achieve more accurate global geometry and realistic renderings than the existing methods. FuseGS introduces three key innovations. First, to overcome low geometric accuracy in image-only methods, we propose a novel LiDAR-guided Gaussian initialization method to ensure strong geometric supervision. Second, to deal with the difficulty of integrating 2-D VLMs semantics into 3-D space, we propose a novel category-specific dual-cue refinement method to improve semantics and geometry alignment. Third, to remove rendering artifacts caused by dynamic objects, we introduce a novel semantic-based adaptive density control method to effectively maintain static background reconstruction. To demonstrate the robustness of our framework, we evaluate FuseGS using both self-collected data from handheld and uncrewed aerial vehicle platforms, as well as public datasets. Compared to the baseline 3-D GS method, FuseGS unifies joint optimization of photometric, geometric, and static-aware semantics, resulting in 50% improvement in geometric accuracy and 4.8 dB improvement in rendering quality. The method achieves 67.43% mean intersection over union without manual annotation. Ultimately, FuseGS has demonstrated excellent potential for Earth observation applications, such as smart cities and autonomous driving.
author: Yuchen Li, Jing Li, Kai Qin, Haitao Wu, Yin Gao, Banghui Yang

title: Beyond Signal-to-Clutter Ratio: A Multifactor Detectability Framework for Infrared Dim Small Targets
content: Detecting infrared dim small targets remains a significant challenge due to their extremely low signal strength submerged in complex background clutter and noise. Existing evaluation metrics, primarily the signal-to-clutter ratio (SCR), treat backgrounds merely as statistical noise, failing to capture structural textures or target-like interferences. This inadequacy hinders the robust assessment and development of detection algorithms. To address this limitation, we propose a novel multifactor detectability framework that goes beyond simple contrast measures. Our approach systematically decomposes and quantifies detection difficulty through three key dimensions: target salience, background complexity, and target confusion. By integrating these quantifiable factors, we derive a comprehensive and computable target detectability (TD) metric. Extensive experiments conducted on diverse real-world datasets demonstrate that our TD metric aligns significantly better with actual detection challenges than traditional indicators. Specifically, the proposed metric exhibits a much stronger correlation with the detection performance (Area under the curve) of popular algorithms compared to SCR, effectively revealing difficulty variations that SCR overlooks. This framework can provide a more accurate and robust standard for assessing data quality and task difficulty, offering critical guidance for objective benchmarking and the targeted optimization of detection algorithms in sophisticated infrared surveillance scenarios.
author: Erwei Zhao, Bo Liu, Zixu Huang, Yinan Xing

title: Mapping of Fire Perimeter and Reconstruction of Prescribed Grassfire Evolution Using Thermal Imagery From a Low-Altitude UAS
content: Accurate mapping of fire perimeters and reconstruction of fire evolution are essential for fire behavior analysis, fire spread modeling and prediction, and wildland fire situational awareness. In this article, we present a new method for generating unitemporal fire perimeter maps and reconstructing grassfire evolution using low-altitude, multitemporal thermal infrared (TIR) imagery from a small unmanned aircraft system (UAS). The proposed method is novel in its ability to correct spatiotemporal variations of fire perimeters caused by longitudinal and lateral overlapping in structure-from-motion-stitched orthomosaics. It offers a simple and robust approach for mapping a large wildland fire using only a single UAS. In addition, this approach significantly reduces manual processing efforts while improving the temporal fidelity of fire representation, providing a cost-effective solution to produce spatiotemporally consistent fire representations to support modeling, analysis, and fire operations. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated and evaluated using a TIR UAS dataset collected during a 30-acre prescribed grassfire at the Anderson County Prairie Preserve, Kansas.
author: Zhenghao Lin, Haiyang Chao, Saket Gowravaram, Tiebiao Zhao, Sheena Parsons, Ming Xin, Xiaolin Hu, Carlton J. Leuschen

title: SSFDiffusion: A Degradation-Aware Diffusion Model for Ocean Sound Speed Field Super-Resolution
content: Ocean sound speed field (SSF) is a fundamental geophysical parameter in underwater acoustics and ocean observation, as it plays a critical role in applications, such as sonar imaging, underwater communication, and navigation. However, its low spatial resolution limits the performance of these applications in practical scenarios.Super-resolution (SR) techniques provide an effective means to reconstruct high-resolution SSFs from low-resolution observations.Nevertheless, most existing SR methods assume a simple, static degradation process, which overlooks the complex and unknown degradation and noise patterns inherent in real marine environments.This oversimplification often results in limited generalization and suboptimal performance in real-world applications.To overcome this limitation, we propose SSFDiffusion, a degradation-aware diffusion model that explicitly models the coupled effects of global degradation and local noise through the following three core components: first, a dynamic denoising network adaptive to varying noise levels, second, a directional multiscale transformer for modeling global degradation and local noise, and third, an alternating optimization strategy that balances degradation correction and noise suppression to prevent artifact accumulation.Extensive experiments on the South China Sea and GDCSM_Argo datasets demonstrate that SSFDiffusion consistently outperforms seven state-of-the-art SR methods across five key quantitative metrics. Notably, our method achieves up to 48.52 dB peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) and an order-of-magnitude root mean square error (RMSE) reduction.These experimental results indicate that SSFDiffusion enhances both the spatial fidelity and practical applicability of reconstructed SSF data, benefiting oceanographic research and underwater acoustic applications.
author: Yi Liu, Xinhai Chen, Gen Zhang, Qingyang Zhang, Qinglin Wang, Jie Liu

title: Land surface energy balance modeling over rotational croplands: The added value of remotely sensed latent heat-leaf area coupling

title: Chip Industry Week In Review
content:
Acquisitions and business pivots

  • Teradyne acquired Israel-based TestInsight, a semiconductor test provider with pattern conversion, validation, and virtual test capabilities.
  • Credo plans to acquire DustPhotonics, a developer of silicon photonics PICs for optical transceivers.
  • Molex plans to acquire Teramount, a provider of detachable, passive-alignment fiber-to-chip connectivity solutions for CPO.
  • Allbirds sold its footwear assets and will now become an integrated GPU-as-a-Service and AI-native cloud solutions provider called NewBird AI.

Advanced nodes

  • Rapidus will receive an additional US $3.9B from the Japanese government for semiconductor projects, covering both advanced logic manufacturing and 2nm chiplet/package technology. The company also opened an Analysis Center and Chiplet Solutions facility in Hokkaido, aiming for 2027 mass production.

By the numbers

  • Morningstar expects the chip industry will reach $1.1T of revenue in 2026, earlier than previously predicted, with no sign of an AI slowdown.
  • Meeting AI demand is another matter, though. The boom is forcing a structural shift in the memory market, with 62% of manufacturers reporting limited supplies and longer lead times, 82% report higher prices, and only 14% predict that conditions will get better in the next 6 months, reports the Global Electronics Association.
  • And after 10 quarters of growth, smartphone shipments declined 4.1% in the first quarter, driven by memory supply constraints, per IDC.
  • Meanwhile, 80 startups raised over $8.4B, with massive rounds for AI, EDA and manufacturing, in the latest chip industry quarterly funding report.
  • Financials: ASML, Aixtron, TSMC (1st quarter).

Geopolitics

  • Bromine, used to produce IC-grade hydrogen bromide gas used in chip etching, is an emerging supply risk due to the war.
  • Adding to the wider global supply squeeze, helium exports will now require special approval from Russia, the world’s third-largest producer.

Semiconductor agents

Cadence expanded its portfolio of ChipStack AI agents with a head agent designed to orchestrate all aspects of semiconductor and system design, along with agents specialized for custom and analog, and for digital implementation and signoff. Cadence is also collaborating with Nvidia to combine agentic AI, physics-based simulation, and digital twins for semiconductor and physical AI engineering, and with Google to integrate Super Agent with Gemini on Google Cloud.

Fig.1: Cadence CEO Anirudh Devgan and Jensen Huang discussing AI’s impact at CadenceLive this week.

AI tracker

  •  AI use is increasing across US workplaces, improving employee productivity and coinciding with more hiring, layoffs, and disruption, but it has not yet fundamentally transformed how most work gets done, says a Gallup poll.
  • Meanwhile, IDC predicts AI spending will grow five-fold from 2024 to 2029 in Asia/Pacific.
  • Stanford’s AI Index Report warns of the broadening real-world impact of AI’s rapid progress this year, including a widening gap between AI’s abilities and preparations to manage it, heightened concerns around energy usage, security, and the level of labor disruption.

Fig.2: Accelerating AI capability. Source: Stanford 2026 AI Index Report [Ref. 1]

Events and Further Reading

Global

Americas

  • Advantest opened a new innovation center in San Jose, CA, offering clean rooms and the latest test technologies and test insertions for advanced packaging and complex device architectures. The company is opening another center in Sunnyvale, CA, this summer.
  • SEMI and McKinsey are conducting an anonymous semiconductor supply chain survey, with input due by May 8.
  • SCREEN’s new R&D center at NY Creates’ Albany nanotech complex opened.
  • The US government is awarding $5.4M toward five gallium recovery projects.
  • The Northeast Microelectronics Coalition is giving out $1M to 8 startups for AI, photonics, and RF systems.
  • Intel Foundry hired Shawn Han as VP/GM of Foundry Services. Han most recently oversaw sales at Samsung Foundry.

Europe

  • Woopix broke ground on its new cleanroom in Tenerife, Spain, for its wavefront phase imaging metrology.
  • The EuroHPC JU inaugurated a new photonic quantum computer in France.
  • The UK Semiconductor Centre established its headquarters at the Institute of Physics in King’s Cross, London.

Asia

  • Air Liquide plans to spend €200M for two new industrial gas production units in Hiroshima, Japan.
  • JSR has established a planarization process solutions research center in Hsinchu, Taiwan.
  • NAND flash memory chip maker YMTC will complete a new Chinese factory this year, plus build two more with the capacity of 100,000 wafers per month, reports Reuters.
  • Alpha and Omega Semiconductor started production in its new power device OSAT facility in Sanand, Gujarat.

In-Depth

Semiconductor Engineering published the Manufacturing, Packaging and Materials newsletter this week:

  • Special Report: Panel-Level Packaging’s Second Wave Meets Engineering Reality
  • Chiplet Standards Aim For Plug-n-Play
  • Silicon Photonics Lights The Way To More Efficient Data Centers

Plus:

  • Tech Talk: Why More CPUs Are Needed For Agentic AI
  • AI Growing Impact On Chip Design And EDA Tools
  • EDA And IP Numbers Up Again
  • Quarterly Report: Startup Funding: Q126

Reports and Deals

More deals, launches

  • Inspection and metrology company Camtek plans to acquire Visual Layer, an Israel-based AI visual analytics.
  • Rebellions, SKT, and Arm are teaming up on an AI server that combines Arm AGI CPU with Rebellions’ AI chips.
  • Vexlum and Menlo Systems are collaborating on a modular photonics source for ultra-precise optical atom clock timekeeping.
  • SNIA launched the MRAM Alliance SIG to promote MRAM adoption across a broad range of applications.
  • SF-based VFabTech launched this week, focusing on semiconductor fab development, cleanroom planning, workforce training, and other semiconductor capacity expansion activities.

Fundings

  • Terra Quantum plans to go public.
  • AlixLabs added ~$1.1M in funding to further its ALE pitch-splitting tech.
  • CamGraPhIC will receive a €211M from Italy to develop graphene-based optical I/O.
  • Sygaldry raised $139M to build quantum-accelerated AI servers.
  • Pixel Photonics raised ~$16M for its waveguide-integrated superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors.
  • Peak Quantum raised ~$2.6M to make superconducting quantum chips with built-in error protection.
  • nEye.ai raised $80M for reconfigurable optical circuit switching interconnects that combine silicon photonics with MEMS technology.

Reports

  • EDA and semiconductor IP revenue grew 10.3% in Q4 2025 to $5.5B, according to the ESD Alliance. While this continues a years-long double-digit run, the Q4 growth picture was more nuanced.
  • 2025 wafer fab equipment revenues grew 12% YoY, hitting $143B (Counterpoint)
  • Global quantum computing market to double by 2028 (QED-C)
  • Nvidia’s global AI empire partnerships (FDI Intelligence)
  • Scope 3-Category 1 Greenhouse Gas Emissions in Electronics (GEA)
  • $48B Market for Photonic IC-Based Transceivers by 2036 (IDTechEx)
  • 1/2 of new warehouses will be human-optional facilities by 2030 (Gartner)

Opinions

  • Reining in the Export Control Arms Race (CSIS)
  • Here’s how to get the $7T AI HW buildout right (WEF)
  • The Right Way to Sell Chips to China (AI Frontiers)
  • China’s economic ambitions and its 5-Year Plan (Brookings)
  • Battle for AI Dominance, Computing Power Is the New High Ground (AEI)
  • Has The Iran War Triggered Another Semiconductor Crisis (Eurasia Review)

New Technologies

Onto Innovation‘s recently released Dragonfly G5 inspection platform is now qualified for 2.5D AI packaging applications.

Athena introduced a new agentic AI tool, FabOrchestrator, for smart manufacturing in the fab and other environments.

In a recent tech talk, D2S‘ Aki Fujimura talks about how far the industry has come in terms of computational resources and multi-beam mask writing to facilitate the move to curvilinear masks and design.

Nvidia released a family of open AI models for quantum processor calibration and quantum error-correction decoding.

Meta shared details of its second-gen Meta Training and Inference Accelerator (MTIA) chip designed for large-scale infrastructure. Meta will also partner with Broadcom to co-develop multiple generations of the MTIA and deploy over 1GW of capacity using Broadcom’s XPU platform and Ethernet technologies.

Faraday Technology announced IP that integrates SST eFlash controller and BiST with analog and high-speed interface PHY IPs for UMC‘s 28nm platform.

Panmnesia will be sampling a PCIe 6.4-CXL 3.2 fusion switch chip with port-based routing. It’s also expanding into controller and switch SoCs for UALink and Ethernet-based interconnects as part of a $10M R&D project.

NASA selected Synopsys and Electro Magnetic Applications to research Artemis spacesuit triboelectrification, electrical charging, and electrostatic discharge resulting from exposure to the lunar environment.

Workforce, Education

Teenagers can now suit up in a 16,500 sf. full cleanroom environment in a Phoenix school district, preparing students for jobs in the fab, packaging facilities as well as robotics training.

SEMI is extending its SEMIquest hands-on immersion semiconductor experience at the Arizona Science Center through May 31.

The Oregon government awarded over $8.5M in grants to eight recipients to bolster the state’s semiconductor talent pipeline, covering activities such as building a mock fab room, dual credit opportunities in high schools, and internships.

Mitsubishi Chemical, PsiQuantum, and the U. of Tokyo joined forces on training and education for Japan’s quantum workforce.

UT Dallas was awarded a $700k grant from a Texas state fund, targeted for a semiconductor training cleanroom project.

Research

DARPA launched the Heterogeneous Architectures for Quantum program to design architectures that combine different qubit types, each selected for what it does best, into a single system. In addition to software frameworks and circuit compilers, the effort aims to develop interconnects that enable communication between different types of qubits.

UCLA-led researchers discovered that metallic theta-phase tantalum nitride conducted heat nearly 3x better than copper or silver.

3D electron diffraction provides a more comprehensive nanoscale picture of how organic solar-cell materials are structured within a TEM, helping FAU researchers better connect molecular arrangement to device performance.

Computer architects may find this Hall of Fame report useful, showing a ranking of top research papers across the top conferences and links to their publications. Onur Mutlu from ETH Zurich took the top spot.

And..

  • Neural Computers (Meta AI, KAUST)
  • Photonic chip packaging for extreme environments (NIST et al.)
  • New materials show promise for parallel computing (Clemson)
  • Characterization of Electr. Stress-Induced Changes in Multilayer MoS2 (NIST)
  • Capacitor-free next-gen DRAM structure based on oxide semiconductors (ETRI)
  • Trilinear CIM Architecture for Energy-Efficient Transformer Accel. (Penn State)
  • Laser absorption spectroscopy: a breakthrough in plasma etch endpoint detection for EUV and ALE patterning process (Lam Research)

 

Security

A recent Global Electronics Association report warns that the FCC’s March 2026 proposal to classify consumer routers made abroad as covered equipment (meaning a security risk) could strain the US router market by hiking costs, tightening supply, and delaying adoption of newer Wi-Fi technology. Meanwhile, Netgear routers were granted conditional approval and are exempt from the router ban.

NIST describes new techniques for improving system security and resilience in bus-connected hardware, including data fusion techniques and “repurposing component firmware as a network of forensic units.”

AI security

  • IBM rolled out its Autonomous Security, a multi-agent‑powered service aimed at automating vulnerability remediation at machine speed.
  • ETH Zurich professor Florian Tramèr discusses why Claude Mythos makes cyber attacks easier, with “a single hacker can suddenly try out thousands of variants.”
  • Meanwhile, OpenAI is expanding access to its Trusted Access for Cyber program so select security teams can use advanced models, like GPT-5.4-Cyber.
  • Cisco is in preliminary talks to acquire AI security startup Astrix for ~$250M, reports The Information.

New alerts

  • AMD issued a high severity alert on incorrect use of the LocateProtocol Service.
  • Find this week’s CISA alerts this week here, and NIST alerts here.

Security Research

  • Deductive System for Contract Satisfaction Proofs (CISPA, Harvard, MPI-SP)
  • HW-Level Governance of AI Compute (U. of Chester)
  • Cryptography-Inspired IP Protection through Fine-Grain HW Redaction (UF)
  • Side-Channel-Protected Stream Cipher HW for 6G Systems (Tsinghua)
  • Security Analysis of Universal Circuits as a Mechanism for HW Obfuscation (U. of Idaho, Carnegie Mellon)

Vehicles, Batteries

The Automotive Edge Computing Consortium rolled out a data-first architecture, utilizing a distributed, tiered model that processes and moves data across multiple layers. “By combining diverse communication methods — including cellular, Wi-Fi, and inter-vehicle data transfer — with distributed computing across vehicles, edge infrastructure, and cloud platforms, the data-first architecture offers a practical path forward for managing automotive data at scale,” stated Ryokichi Onishi, AECC board chairperson, in the consortium’s news release.

Imec examined Arm’s role in the Automotive Chiplet Program, and also added a new chip partner, Silicon Box.

Infineon retained its leadership position as the top automotive semiconductor supplier, with a 2025 overall market share of ~13% and a MCU share of 36%. Also, the company’s rad-hard solutions were part of NASA’s Artemis II mission in space.

Bosch and Qualcomm are expanding their partnership to include ADAS and emerging centralized vehicle architectures.

Samsung Electro-Mechanics rolled out the ultra-high-voltage MLCCs for xEV high- voltage powertrains for BEVs and PHEVs.

AVs

  • AV-AI startup Wayve received a $60M infusion from AMD, Arm, and Qualcomm to fund its ADAS and automated driving platform.
  • The robotaxi market will hit $167B by 2035, with 3.6M vehicles, predicts Counterpoint.
  • After 18 months of testing, the Dutch vehicle authority will allow Tesla‘s full self-driving supervised system with the driver remaining responsible and in control, and will also seek EU-wide approval.
  • Elon Musk announced the Tesla AI design team taped out its AI15 self-driving chip.

EVs, batteries

  • Slate Auto, a Jeff Bezos-backed startup, raised $650M for its electric truck, scheduled to be rolled out later this year. The advertised price point in the mid-$20,000 range garnered more than 160,000 reservations.
  • In Q1, EV sales fell 27% versus the prior year, reports Cox Automotive, and accounted for 5.8% of total new car sales.
  • Doug Field, Ford‘s top EV head, is leaving as part of a reorg.
  • Rivian is teaming up with Redwood Materials on a repurposed battery energy storage system for Rivian’s manufacturing plant.
  • A KIST-led team created a 15 minute trimethyl phosphate treatment to secure a more moisture-tolerant magnesium-metal battery electrodes, potentially lowering rechargeable magnesium battery manufacturing costs.

Vehicle research, reports

  • End-to-End Language-Driven Autonomous Driving (Texas Tech, Bosch)
  • IME Be the Path for Printed Electronics to Break into Automotives (IDTechEx)
  • DNN-Based Roadwork Detection for Autonomous Driving (FU Berlin)
  • Drone Dataset for Complex Vehicle-VRU Interactions within Mixed Traffic
  • Battery State-of-Health Using Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (Texas Tech)
  • How Russia Is Building a Sovereign Drone Ecosystem for AI-Driven Autonomy (CSIS)
  • Range and Lifetime Extension of EVs via Cell-Level Inverters (Berkeley, Chalmers, BMW)
  • Precise intrinsic calibration and geometrical stability analysis of automotive solid-state LiDAR sensors over temperature and lifetime (UAS et al.)

Trending Video

State Of The Market For Edge Silicon: What makes one AI chip better than another? Steve Roddy, chief marketing officer at Quadric, talks about the need to balance performance and efficiency with flexibility for different applications, what makes one chip better than another, and how to determine the best combination of processing elements.

title: Emulation-based SoC Security Verification (U. of Florida)
content:
A new technical paper, “Emulation-based System-on-Chip Security Verification: Challenges and Opportunities,” was published by researchers at University of Florida.

Abstract
“Increasing system-on-chip (SoC) heterogeneity, deep hardware/software integration, and the proliferation of third-party intellectual property (IP) have brought security validation to the forefront of semiconductor design. While simulation and formal verification remain indispensable, they often struggle to expose vulnerabilities that emerge only under realistic execution conditions, long software-driven interactions, and adversarial stimuli. In this context, hardware emulation is emerging as an increasingly important pre-silicon verification technology because it enables higher-throughput execution of RTL designs under realistic hardware/software workloads while preserving sufficient fidelity for security-oriented analysis.

This paper presents a comprehensive survey and perspective on emulation-based security verification and validation. We organize the landscape of prior work across assertion-based security checking, coverage-driven exploration, adversarial testing, information-flow tracking, fault injection, and side-channel-oriented evaluation. We provide a structured view of emulation-enabled security verification workflows, including instrumentation, stimulus generation, runtime monitoring, and evidence-driven analysis. We also examine practical challenges related to observability, scalability, property specification, and the definition of security-oriented coverage metrics for emulation-based verification. Finally, we discuss emerging directions such as AI-assisted emulation, digital security twins, chiplet-scale security exploration, automated vulnerability assessment, and cloud-scale secure emulation. Overall, this paper positions emulation as a promising foundation for the next generation of pre-silicon hardware security assurance.”

Find the paper here. April 2026.

arXiv:2604.15073. Authors: Tanvir Rahman, Shuvagata Saha, Ahmed Y. Alhurubi, Sujan Kumar Saha, Farimah Farahmandi, Mark Tehranipoor
